docs.title: CAPI + vSphere UAT Cluster
docs.text: This documentation covers a complete, field-tested setup of a Kubernetes workload cluster on vSphere using Cluster API.

docs.title: High-level outcome
docs.text:
• One control-plane node and N worker nodes created via CAPI MachineDeployments.
  • API Server VIP provided by kube-vip (192.168.35.100) and a DNS record kube-api-server.uat.local.
  • Node IPs allocated from InClusterIPPool (tmsnw-pool) via addressesFromPools.
  • Guest networking stabilized by defining the default route (and metric) in the CAPV network device spec.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
Cluster API is extremely deterministic when three pillars are correct:

  1. vSphere primitives are accurate (template, folder, resource pool, datastore, network)
  2. Guest bootstrap is healthy (cloud-init runs, routes exist, kubeadm succeeds)
  3. Reachability exists between:
  4. management cluster -> workload VIP:6443
  5. workload nodes -> VIP:6443 (for kubeadm join)
  6. workload nodes -> DNS/NTP/package repos (if needed)

If any of those are missing, you’ll see the classic pattern: - Control plane VM exists and is ready in CAPV - Machines/VSphereMachines for workers exist but remain Pending - Controllers log cluster is not reachable: ... connect: no route to host

Continue to Architecture to understand the data plane + control plane flows.

docs.title: Cluster Yaml Generation
docs.text:
Use the environment variables created for the vcenter previously on vSphere Environment Variables Bash

source /etc/capi-vsphere.env

docs.title: Network ResourceSet creation for the Network inside the clusters.
docs.text:
1. Download the calico manifest file Bash
    curl -fsSLo calico.yaml https://docs.tigera.io/calico/latest/manifests/calico.yaml
  2. Enable CALICO_IPV4POOL_CIDR
Bash
sed -i 's#192\.168\.0\.0/16#10.244.0.0/16#g' calico.yaml  
sed -i 's/# - name: CALICO_IPV4POOL_CIDR/- name: CALICO_IPV4POOL_CIDR/' calico.yaml

sed -i 's/#   value: "10.244.0.0\/16"/  value: "10.244.0.0\/16"/' calico.yaml

Note

Here 10.244.0.0/16 is defined on the environment variables as well.

  1. Disable IP (best for VMWare Environments) Bash
    sed -i 's/"Always"/"Never"/' calico.yaml
  2. Enable VXLAN overlay (recommended for vSphere networks) Bash
    sed -i '/CALICO_IPV4POOL_IPIP/a\            - name: CALICO_IPV4POOL_VXLAN\n              value: "Always"' calico.yaml
  3. Verify the result Bash
    grep -n CALICO_IPV4POOL calico.yaml
  4. Recreate the ConfigMap Bash
    kubectl create configmap calico-manifest -n default --from-file=calico.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply --server-side -f -
  5. Create the cluster resource set: calico-crs.yaml YAML
    apiVersion: addons.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta2
kind: ClusterResourceSet
metadata:
  name: calico-crs
  namespace: default
spec:
  strategy: Reconcile
  clusterSelector:
    matchLabels:
      cni: calico
  resources:
    - kind: ConfigMap
      name: calico-manifest
  6. Apply the crs created above Bash
    kubectl apply -f calico-crs.yaml

docs.title: Metrics Server Integration for Resources
docs.text:
1. Download the Metrics server latest manifest file. Bash
    wget https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml -O metrics-server.yaml
  2. Update the manifest file to add the insecure tls details YAML
    - --kubelet-insecure-tls
- --kubelet-preferred-address-types=InternalIP,Hostname,InternalDNS,ExternalDNS,ExternalIP

Tip

  1. Create the configmap for the metrics server Bash
    kubectl create configmap metrics-server-manifest -n default --from-file=/root/capicluster/addons/metrics-server/metrics-server.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply --server-side -f -
  2. Create the Cluster Resource Defination for the deployment in metrics-server-crs.yaml YAML
    apiVersion: addons.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta2
kind: ClusterResourceSet
metadata:
  name: metrics-server-crs
  namespace: default
spec:
  strategy: Reconcile
  clusterSelector:
    matchLabels:
      metrics: enabled
  resources:
    - kind: ConfigMap
      name: metrics-server-manifest
  3. Apply the Cluster Resource Set Bash
    kubectl apply -f metrics-server-crs.yaml

docs.title: Cluster Generation
docs.text:
Generate the cluster from the command: Bash

clusterctl generate cluster "${CLUSTER_NAME}" \
 --kubernetes-version "${KUBERNETES_VERSION}" \
 --control-plane-machine-count "${CONTROL_PLANE_MACHINE_COUNT}" \
 --worker-machine-count "${WORKER_MACHINE_COUNT}" \
 --infrastructure vsphere \
| yq '

  # -------------------------
  # Cluster labels for addons
  # -------------------------
  (select(.kind=="Cluster" and .metadata.name==strenv(CLUSTER_NAME)).metadata.labels.cni) = "calico" |
    (select(.kind=="Cluster" and .metadata.name==strenv(CLUSTER_NAME)).metadata.labels.metrics) = "enabled" |

  # -------------------------
  # Kubernetes networking
  # -------------------------
  (select(.kind=="Cluster" and .metadata.name==strenv(CLUSTER_NAME)).spec.clusterNetwork.pods.cidrBlocks) =
  [strenv(POD_CIDR)]
  |

  (select(.kind=="Cluster" and .metadata.name==strenv(CLUSTER_NAME)).spec.clusterNetwork.services.cidrBlocks) =
  [strenv(SERVICE_CIDR)]

  |

  # -------------------------
  # VIP endpoint
  # -------------------------
  (select(.kind=="VSphereCluster" and .metadata.name==strenv(CLUSTER_NAME))
    .spec.controlPlaneEndpoint.host) =
    strenv(CONTROL_PLANE_ENDPOINT_HOST)

  |

  (select(.kind=="KubeadmControlPlane" and .metadata.name==strenv(CLUSTER_NAME))
    .spec.kubeadmConfigSpec.files[]
      | select(.path=="/etc/kube-vip.hosts").content) =
  ("127.0.0.1 localhost kubernetes\n" +
   strenv(CONTROL_PLANE_ENDPOINT_IP) + " " +
   strenv(CONTROL_PLANE_ENDPOINT_HOST) + "\n")

  |

  # -------------------------
  # Control plane network
  # -------------------------
  (select(.kind=="VSphereMachineTemplate"
    and .metadata.name==strenv(CLUSTER_NAME))
    .spec.template.spec.network.devices[0]) = {
      "networkName": strenv(VSPHERE_NETWORK),
      "dhcp4": false,
      "gateway4": strenv(NODE_GATEWAY),
      "nameservers": [
        strenv(NODE_DNS_1),
        strenv(NODE_DNS_2),
        strenv(NODE_DNS_3)
      ],
      "addressesFromPools": [{
        "apiGroup": "ipam.cluster.x-k8s.io",
        "kind": "InClusterIPPool",
        "name": strenv(IP_POOL_NAME)
      }]
  }

  |

  # -------------------------
  # Worker network
  # -------------------------
  (select(.kind=="VSphereMachineTemplate"
    and .metadata.name==(strenv(CLUSTER_NAME) + "-worker"))
    .spec.template.spec.network.devices[0]) = {
      "networkName": strenv(VSPHERE_NETWORK),
      "dhcp4": false,
      "gateway4": strenv(NODE_GATEWAY),
      "nameservers": [
        strenv(NODE_DNS_1),
        strenv(NODE_DNS_2),
        strenv(NODE_DNS_3)
      ],
      "addressesFromPools": [{
        "apiGroup": "ipam.cluster.x-k8s.io",
        "kind": "InClusterIPPool",
        "name": strenv(IP_POOL_NAME)
      }]
  }

' > cluster.yaml

Apply the yaml generated Bash

kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f cluster.yaml

kubectl apply -f cluster.yaml

kubectl get machines -owide

Tip

This would take around 10 - 15 minutes for the cluster configurations.

docs.title: Cluster Health Verification
docs.text:
Verify cluster components after creation.

```bash kubectl get nodes -o wide --kubeconfig= kubectl get pods -A --kubeconfig= kubectl get machines -A kubectl get vspheremachines -A

Expected results:

  • All nodes Ready

  • Calico pods running

  • CoreDNS running

  • kube-vip running

  • vSphere CPI running

  • Metrics Server pods running

docs.text:
Bash
yum update -y

hostnamectl set-hostname clustertemplate

docs.title: Cloud init Installation
docs.text:
Bash
yum install -y cloud-init cloud-utils-growpart gdisk jq

Start all the services: Bash

systemctl enable cloud-init-local.service
systemctl enable cloud-init.service
systemctl enable cloud-config.service
systemctl enable cloud-final.service
systemctl start cloud-init.service
systemctl start cloud-init-local.service
systemctl start cloud-config.service
systemctl start cloud-final.service

Configure the cloud init vmware booting process Bash

sudo tee /etc/cloud/cloud.cfg.d/99-vmware.cfg >/dev/null <<'EOF'
datasource_list: [ Vmware, OVF, NoCloud, ConfigureDrive, None ]
EOF

docs.title: Network manager enabled
docs.text:
Bash
systemctl enable --now NetworkManager

docs.title: Containerd Installed
docs.text:
Bash
sudo yum config-manager --add-repo https://download.docker.com/linux/centos/docker-ce.repo
sudo yum install -y containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin
sudo systemctl enable --now docker
sudo systemctl status docker
sudo systemctl enable --now containerd
sudo mkdir -p /etc/containerd
sudo containerd config default | sudo tee /etc/containerd/config.toml >/dev/null

docs.title: VMWare Tools configurations
docs.text:
Bash
yum install -y open-vm-tools 
systemctl enable --now vmtoolsd
systemctl status vmtoolsd

docs.title: Kubernetes Packages installed:
docs.text:
Bash
sudo tee /etc/yum.repos.d/kubernetes.repo >/dev/null <<'EOF'
[kubernetes]
name=Kubernetes
baseurl=https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.33/rpm/
enabled=1
gpgcheck=1
gpgkey=https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.33/rpm/repodata/repomd.xml.key
EOF
sudo yum install -y kubelet kubeadm kubectl
sudo systemctl enable kubelet

docs.text:
Bash
sudo sed -i 's/SystemdCgroup = false/SystemdCgroup = true/' /etc/containerd/config.toml

sudo systemctl restart containerd

docs.title: Kernel Sysctl update
docs.text:
Bash
sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-kubernetes-cri.conf >/dev/null <<'EOF'
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-iptables  = 1
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-ip6tables = 1
net.ipv4.ip_forward                 = 1
EOF
sudo sysctl --system

docs.title: Firewalld configuration
docs.text:
Bash
sudo systemctl is-active firewalld
sudo systemctl disable --now firewalld

Note

Since we are disabling the firewalld now, we would enable the firewalld using the cloud-init later on while creating the cluster. Clusterctl Generation Command

docs.text:
Bash
yum install -y versionlock
yum versionlock kubectl kubeadm kubelet

docs.text:
Bash
lsmod | egrep 'br_netfilter|overlay'
containerd --version
kubeadm version
kubelet --version

docs.title: Final Cleanup
docs.text:
Bash
sudo systemctl-reload-machine-id
sudo systemctl stop kubelet 2>/dev/null || true
sudo cloud-init clean --logs
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/cloud/*
sudo rm -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_*
sudo rm -f /etc/machine-id
sudo truncate -s 0 /var/log/wtmp /var/log/btmp || true
sudo sync
sudo poweroff

docs.title: Initialize the Cluster
docs.text:
Info

To initialize the cluster api, vSphere, clusterctl requires the following variables to be set in ~/.cluster-api/clusterctl.yaml as following:

  1. Create the Configuration file in the user's home directory. Bash

    mkdir -p ~/.cluster-api
vim ~/.cluster-api/clusterctl.yaml

  2. Add the Contents inside the cluster.yaml file. YAML

    providers:
  - name: cluster-api
    type: CoreProvider
    url: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/v1.12.2/core-components.yaml

  - name: kubeadm
    type: BootstrapProvider
    url: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/v1.12.2/bootstrap-components.yaml

  - name: kubeadm
    type: ControlPlaneProvider
    url: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/v1.12.2/control-plane-components.yaml

  - name: vsphere
    type: InfrastructureProvider
    url: https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api-provider-vsphere/releases/v1.15.2/infrastructure-components.yaml

Tip

When the following command is executed:

clusterctl init clusterctl performs the following: 1. Reads the provider configuration from: ~/.cluster-api/clusterctl.yaml 2. Downloads the provider manifests from the specified URLs. 3. Installs the required components into the management cluster, including:

Text Only
- CRDs
- Controllers
- Webhooks
- RBAC permissions
  1. The controllers begin watching Cluster API resources and reconciling infrastructure state.

Note

Initialize the Cluster API providers using the following command:

Bash
clusterctl init --config ~/.cluster-api/clusterctl.yaml

  1. Configure the IPAM so that it could be used for provisioning the IP for Clusters which are not in DHCP Network. Bash
    clusterctl init --ipam in-cluster
kubectl get pods -A |grep ipam

Tip

All the pods should be running.

  1. Create the ipool.yaml file so that it could be used by the management cluster while creating the new VM.
YAML
apiVersion: ipam.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha2
kind: InClusterIPPool
metadata:
  name: <ippool Name>
  namespace: default
spec:
  addresses:
  - <Network Start Range>-<Network End Range>
  prefix: <Subnet Mask>
  gateway: <Gateway>

docs.title: Vcenter Environment Variables
docs.text:
The following environment variables are required for deploying Kubernetes clusters using Cluster API Provider vSphere.

  • Create the secure environment file /etc/capi-vsphere.env which would be used for the storing all the details for the vsphere deployment.

Warning

Do not commit real credentials or internal infrastructure values to a public repository. Replace the placeholder values below with the appropriate values for your environment.

Text Only
# vCenter credentials
export VSPHERE_USERNAME='<VCENTER_USERNAME>'
export VSPHERE_PASSWORD='<VCENTER_PASSWORD>'
export VSPHERE_SERVER='<VCENTER_FQDN_OR_IP>'

# vSphere infrastructure details
export VSPHERE_DATACENTER='<DATACENTER_NAME>'
export VSPHERE_DATASTORE='<DATASTORE_NAME>'
export VSPHERE_RESOURCE_POOL='<RESOURCE_POOL_PATH>'
export VSPHERE_FOLDER='<VM_FOLDER_PATH>'
export VSPHERE_NETWORK='<PORTGROUP_NETWORK_NAME>'

# Allow self-signed certificates if required
export VSPHERE_INSECURE='true'

# VM template used for node cloning
export VSPHERE_TEMPLATE='<VM_TEMPLATE_NAME>'

# Kubernetes API endpoint (VIP)
export CONTROL_PLANE_ENDPOINT_IP='<CONTROL_PLANE_VIP_IP>'
export CONTROL_PLANE_ENDPOINT_HOST='<CONTROL_PLANE_VIP_DNS>'

# Workload cluster metadata
export CLUSTER_NAME='<CLUSTER_NAME>'
export KUBERNETES_VERSION='<KUBERNETES_VERSION>'
export CONTROL_PLANE_MACHINE_COUNT='<CONTROL_PLANE_NODE_COUNT>'
export WORKER_MACHINE_COUNT='<WORKER_NODE_COUNT>'

# Network interface used by kube-vip
export VIP_NETWORK_INTERFACE='<NETWORK_INTERFACE_NAME>'

# vCenter TLS certificate thumbprint
export VSPHERE_TLS_THUMBPRINT='<VCENTER_TLS_THUMBPRINT>'

# vSphere Cloud Provider Interface image version
export CPI_IMAGE_K8S_VERSION='<CPI_VERSION>'

# SSH public key injected into nodes
export VSPHERE_SSH_AUTHORIZED_KEY='<SSH_PUBLIC_KEY>'

# Storage policy (optional)
export VSPHERE_STORAGE_POLICY='<STORAGE_POLICY_NAME_OR_EMPTY>'

# Node network configuration
export NODE_GATEWAY='<NETWORK_GATEWAY>'
export NODE_DNS_1='<PRIMARY_DNS_SERVER>'
export NODE_DNS_2='<SECONDARY_DNS_SERVER>'
export NODE_DNS_3='<TERTIARY_DNS_SERVER>'

# Kubernetes networking configuration
export POD_CIDR='<POD_NETWORK_CIDR>'
export SERVICE_CIDR='<SERVICE_NETWORK_CIDR>'

# Cluster API IPAM pool
export IP_POOL_NAME='<IPAM_POOL_NAME>'

Apply the environment variables to the shell for the variables. Bash

source /etc/capi-vsphere.env

docs.title: Govc Environment Variables
docs.text:
Since govc is used for the connecting with the vcenter using the api's. Based on the environment varialbles configured above apply the environment variables.

Bash
export GOVC_URL="https://${VSPHERE_SERVER}"
export GOVC_USERNAME="${VSPHERE_USERNAME}"
export GOVC_PASSWORD="${VSPHERE_PASSWORD}"
export GOVC_INSECURE=1
export GOVC_DATACENTER="${VSPHERE_DATACENTER}"

Tip

Verify the resoures that you have defined on the envrionment variables are correct or not by: Bash

govc datastore.info -ds "${VSPHERE_DATASTORE}"
#List folders to confirm exact spelling/path
govc ls "/${VSPHERE_DATACENTER}/vm"
govc ls "/<DATACENTER_NAME>/host"

docs.title: 1) Management cluster (CAPI management plane)
docs.text:
The management cluster hosts the controllers that reconcile the desired state:

  • capi-controller-manager (core CAPI)
  • capv-controller-manager (Cluster API Provider vSphere)
  • capi-kubeadm-bootstrap-controller-manager
  • capi-kubeadm-control-plane-controller-manager
  • capi-ipam-in-cluster (if using InClusterIPPool / IPAddressClaims)
Text Only
Management Node
│
├── Kind Bootstrap Cluster
│   ├── CAPI Controllers
│   ├── CAPV Controller
│   ├── IPAM Provider
│   └── ClusterResourceSet
│
└── Workload Kubernetes Cluster
    ├── Control Plane Nodes
    ├── Worker Nodes
    ├── Calico CNI
    ├── kube-vip
    ├── vSphere CPI
    └── CoreDNS

docs.title: 2) Workload cluster (your target cluster)
docs.text: Provisioned by the management cluster: - Control plane: KubeadmControlPlane (replicas: 1 in this build) - Workers: MachineDeployment (replicas: 2 in this build)

docs.title: 3) vSphere objects created/consumed
docs.text:
• Template VM: Alma-Clusterconfigtemplate
  • Folder: /Eshare-SRVs/vm/UAT-Servers/ClusterServers
  • ResourcePool: /Eshare-SRVs/host/192.168.150.16/Resources/CAPI_ClusterRP
  • Datastore: R740xd-datastore1
  • Network/Portgroup: TMSNW

docs.title: 4) API VIP (kube-vip)
docs.text:
• VIP: 192.168.35.100
  • DNS: kube-api-server.uat.local -> 192.168.35.100
  • Port: 6443
  • Runs as a static pod on control-plane via /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-vip.yaml
  • Uses ARP mode (vip_arp=true) in this environment.

docs.title: Management cluster -> Workload cluster
docs.text:
• CAPV controllers try to contact https://kube-api-server.uat.local:6443
  • If the VIP is not routable, controllers repeatedly log no route to host and workers won’t join.

docs.title: Workload nodes -> API VIP
docs.text:
• Worker kubeadm join also targets the VIP
  • If the node has no default route (missing gateway), it cannot reach VIP or DNS => join fails

docs.title: Failure modes that look like CAPV issues but are actually networking
docs.text:
• VM has an IP (from IPAM) but cannot ping gateway
  • ip route shows no default via ...
  • nmtui shows gateway empty
  • CAPV logs show cluster is not reachable

docs.title: Why use Cluster API?
docs.text: Cluster API enables declarative Kubernetes cluster lifecycle management.

docs.title: Why use kube-vip?
docs.text:
kube-vip provides a virtual IP for the Kubernetes API server.

This ensures:

  • high availability
  • stable control plane endpoint

docs.title: Why use Calico?
docs.text:
Calico provides:

  • pod networking
  • network policy enforcement
  • scalable overlay networking

docs.title: Why use IPAM?
docs.text: IPAM allows deterministic IP assignment when DHCP is unavailable.

docs.title: Why use a VM template?
docs.text: Templates ensure consistent node provisioning and faster deployment.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text:
📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2026-03-11 Updated Date: Wednesday 11th March 2026 00:13:06 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text: clusterctl init fails or controllers do not start.

docs.title: Possible causes
docs.text:
• Incorrect provider configuration
  • Internet access issues
  • Invalid clusterctl.yaml

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
Bash
clusterctl config repositories  
kubectl get pods -A

docs.title: VMs are not created in vCenter
Symptoms
docs.text: Cluster YAML applied but no VMs appear.

docs.title: Possible causes
docs.text:
• Incorrect template name
  • Invalid resource pool
  • Incorrect folder path
  • Wrong datastore

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
Bash
kubectl get machines -A  
kubectl get vspheremachines -A  
kubectl -n capv-system logs deploy/capv-controller-manager

Verify vCenter objects:

Bash
govc ls "/${VSPHERE_DATACENTER}/vm"

docs.title: VM created but node never joins cluster
Symptoms
docs.text: VM boots but does not appear in Kubernetes nodes.

docs.title: Possible causes
docs.text:
• cloud-init failure
  • missing gateway
  • DNS misconfiguration
  • kubeadm failure

docs.title: Check inside node
docs.text:
Bash
cloud-init status  
journalctl -u kubelet -xe  
ip route  
cat /etc/resolv.conf

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
Bash
kubectl get machines

shows workers stuck in Provisioning.

docs.title: Possible causes
docs.text:
• Control plane unreachable
  • API VIP unreachable
  • network misconfiguration

docs.title: Checks
Symptoms
docs.text:
CAPV logs show:

cluster is not reachable: connect: no route to host

docs.title: Possible causes
docs.text:
• missing default route
  • incorrect gateway
  • kube-vip not running

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
Bash
ip route  
ping <gateway>  
curl -k https://<VIP>:6443/healthz

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text: Pods in calico-system not running.

docs.title: Possible causes
docs.text:
• wrong pod CIDR
  • invalid manifest patch
  • CRS label mismatch

docs.title: Checks
docs.text:
Bash
kubectl get clusterresourcesets  
kubectl get configmap calico-manifest  
kubectl get pods -n calico-system

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text: ImagePullBackOff

docs.title: Cause
docs.text: Incorrect CPI version.

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
Ensure version matches Kubernetes version.

Example:

registry.k8s.io/cloud-pv-vsphere/cloud-provider-vsphere:v1.30.0

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text: Cluster resources remain after deletion.

docs.title: Possible causes
docs.text:
• finalizers
  • orphaned VMs

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
Remove finalizers carefully:

Bash
kubectl patch vspheremachine <name> -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":[]}}'

Warning

Removing finalizers should only be used when normal deletion fails.

docs.title: Environment Validation
docs.text: Before deploying the cluster, verify that all required components are installed and functional.

docs.title: Management Node Validation
docs.text:
Requirement Validation Command Docker docker version Kind kind version kubectl kubectl version --client clusterctl clusterctl version govc govc version vCenter connectivity govc about

Example:

Bash
govc about

docs.title: Template VM Validation
docs.text:
Before converting the VM to a template verify:

cloud-init status containerd --version kubeadm version kubelet --version lsmod | egrep 'overlay|br_netfilter'

Expected results:

  • cloud-init installed
  • containerd installed
  • kubeadm installed
  • kernel modules loaded

docs.title: Tested Versions
docs.text:
This deployment guide has been validated using the following versions.

Component Version Kubernetes v1.33.8 Cluster API v1.12.2 CAPV (Cluster API Provider vSphere) v1.15.2 Calico v3.x Kind latest stable Kubectl matching Kubernetes version AlmaLinux Template AlmaLinux 9 vCenter 7.x / 8.x ESXi 7.x / 8.x

Warning

Mixing incompatible versions of Cluster API, CAPV, and Kubernetes may cause cluster creation failures.

Always verify compatibility using the official documentation before upgrading.

docs.title: 01PreparationPlan
docs.text:
Absolutely. I reviewed your updated CV, and the right move now is not “learning random tools,” but building a layered DevOps foundation so every line in your CV is backed by real conceptual depth and execution confidence. Your CV already spans networking, datacenter, virtualization, Kubernetes, CI/CD, IaC, observability, security, DR, and cloud operations, so the mentoring path should be structured like an engineering capability ladder, not a course list.

Here is how I would mentor you from ground zero to senior-level maturity.

docs.title: 1. The right mindset before tools
docs.text:
A senior DevOps engineer is not someone who knows many tools. A senior DevOps engineer understands:

  • how systems are built

  • how systems fail

  • how to automate repeatable work

  • how to secure and observe systems

  • how to design for recovery, scale, and change

So your learning order should be:

Fundamentals → Systems → Automation → Platforms → Reliability → Architecture

Not:

Terraform → Kubernetes → Jenkins → AWS

That second path creates shallow knowledge. The first path creates senior-level judgment.

docs.title: 2. Your master roadmap
docs.text:
Based on your CV, I would break your mentoring journey into 12 learning pillars:

  1. Linux and Operating System fundamentals

  2. Networking and datacenter fundamentals

  3. Virtualization and compute platforms

  4. Storage fundamentals

  5. Git, scripting, and automation basics

  6. CI/CD engineering fundamentals

  7. Cloud fundamentals, especially AWS

  8. Infrastructure as Code and configuration management

  9. Containers and Kubernetes

  10. Observability, logging, alerting, and SRE basics

  11. Security, IAM, hardening, and secrets

  12. Disaster recovery, architecture, and senior-level design thinking

This is the exact order I would teach you.

docs.title: 3. Phase 0 — Ground zero foundation
docs.text: Before DevOps, you need to be strong in these core ideas.

docs.title: 3.1 Linux fundamentals
docs.text:
~~You cannot be strong in DevOps without being dangerous on Linux.~~

~~You must deeply understand:~~

  • ~~file system structure: /etc, /var, /home, /usr, /opt, /tmp~~

  • ~~users, groups, permissions, ownership~~

  • ~~processes, threads, signals~~

  • ~~systemd and service lifecycle~~

  • packages and repositories

  • networking commands

  • logs and troubleshooting

  • shell basics

  • cron and scheduling

  • disks, mounts, partitions, LVM basics

  • SSH, SCP, rsync

  • environment variables

  • kernel basics at a practical level

You should be able to answer:

  • What happens when a Linux server boots?

  • How does a service start and stay running?

  • Why does a process become zombie or defunct?

  • Why is disk full even when df and du don’t match?

  • What is the difference between memory usage, cache, and swap?

  • Why is a port open but service unreachable?

Your command muscle memory should include: ls, cd, cp, mv, rm, find, grep, awk, sed, tail, journalctl, systemctl, ss, netstat, ip, curl, wget, tar, rsync, chmod, chown, ps, top, htop, df, du, mount, lsblk, crontab

docs.title: 3.2 Windows basics
docs.text:
Since your CV mentions hybrid infra, don’t ignore Windows.

You need:

  • AD basics

  • DNS and DHCP basics

  • service management

  • RDP

  • PowerShell basics

  • certificate basics

  • event viewer

  • Windows patching and update model

  • Windows server roles

Not because you’ll be a Windows admin, but because real infra is hybrid.

docs.title: 4. Phase 1 — Networking fundamentals
docs.text: Your CV contains routing, firewalls, segmentation, BGP, OSPF, IPSec, GRE, load balancing, DC-DR. This means your networking foundation must be very strong.

docs.title: 4.1 Absolute basics you must master
docs.text:
Learn these until they feel natural:

  • OSI model

  • TCP/IP model

  • IP addressing

  • subnetting

  • CIDR

  • routing

  • switching

  • ARP

  • DNS

  • DHCP

  • NAT

  • TCP vs UDP

  • handshake and session concepts

  • MTU and fragmentation

  • VLANs

  • trunks vs access ports

  • STP basics

  • LACP basics

You should be able to explain:

  • How a browser reaches a website from a client machine

  • How traffic moves inside same VLAN vs different VLAN

  • Why default gateway matters

  • What happens when DNS fails

  • Why asymmetric routing breaks stateful firewalls

docs.title: 4.2 Intermediate networking
docs.text:
Then move into:

  • OSPF areas, adjacency, cost, LSA basics

  • BGP peers, ASNs, path selection basics

  • GRE vs IPSec

  • site-to-site VPN

  • firewall zones and policy flow

  • ACL vs stateful firewall

  • load balancer concepts: L4 vs L7

  • reverse proxy

  • SSL/TLS handshake

  • health checks

  • failover concepts

  • keepalived / VRRP basics

You should be able to design:

  • branch-to-DC secure connectivity

  • app tier / db tier segmentation

  • HA load balancer topology

  • DC-DR routing failover path

docs.title: 4.3 Datacenter networking
docs.text:
Since your CV mentions spine-leaf and DC-DR architecture, these are senior topics. Learn them only after the fundamentals are stable.

Need to understand:

  • why spine-leaf exists

  • east-west vs north-south traffic

  • top-of-rack switches

  • ECMP basics

  • redundancy domains

  • failure domains

  • underlay vs overlay concept

  • firewall insertion points

  • management plane isolation

  • storage traffic separation

  • backup network separation

docs.title: 5. Phase 2 — Compute, virtualization, and infrastructure fundamentals
docs.text: Your CV mentions VMware, ESXi, vCenter, Proxmox, Sangfor, HA clusters, templates, provisioning.

docs.title: 5.1 Start with physical vs virtual thinking
docs.text:
You must understand:

  • what a server is

  • what CPU, RAM, NIC, disk, RAID mean

  • hypervisor type 1 vs type 2

  • VM lifecycle

  • templates and cloning

  • snapshots vs backups

  • overcommitment

  • resource pools

  • HA vs FT vs DRS at conceptual level

  • cluster vs host vs datastore

You should be able to explain:

  • Why virtualization changed datacenter operations

  • Why snapshot is not backup

  • What happens when a host fails in a cluster

  • Why storage latency kills VM performance

docs.title: 5.2 VMware / Proxmox basics
docs.text:
Then learn practical operations:

  • create VM

  • templates

  • vSwitch / distributed switch concepts

  • datastores

  • vmkernel adapters

  • vMotion basics

  • HA basics

  • cluster scheduling concepts

  • host maintenance mode

  • backups and restores

docs.title: 6. Phase 3 — Storage fundamentals
docs.text:
Many DevOps engineers are weak here. Seniors are not.

You need:

  • block vs file vs object storage

  • SAN vs NAS

  • iSCSI basics

  • NFS basics

  • latency, IOPS, throughput

  • RAID levels

  • snapshots, replication, backups

  • consistency concepts

  • database backup vs filesystem backup

  • storage for VMs

  • storage for Kubernetes

Then map it to your stack:

  • SAN for VMs

  • Longhorn / Ceph for K8s

  • MinIO for object storage

  • backup platform like Veeam

You should know:

  • why distributed storage is operationally expensive

  • why backup success does not always mean restore success

  • why storage class design matters in Kubernetes

docs.title: 7. Phase 4 — Git, scripting, and automation basics
docs.text: Before Terraform and Ansible, you need automation thinking.

docs.title: 7.1 Git fundamentals
docs.text:
You must know:

  • repository

  • branch

  • commit

  • merge

  • rebase

  • tag

  • pull request / merge request

  • conflict resolution

  • rollback strategies

  • release tags

  • versioning

A DevOps engineer should treat Git as a system-of-record, not just a code hosting tool.

docs.title: 7.2 Bash and Python basics
docs.text:
From ground zero, I would teach:

  • variables

  • loops

  • conditionals

  • functions

  • exit codes

  • error handling

  • parsing command output

  • file manipulation

  • calling APIs

  • JSON/YAML handling

Why this matters: If you don’t understand scripting, you will use Terraform, Ansible, and Jenkins mechanically instead of intelligently.

docs.title: 8. Phase 5 — CI/CD fundamentals
docs.text: Your CV strongly highlights Jenkins, Git-based releases, pipelines, notifications, artifact promotion, hybrid delivery.

docs.title: 8.1 First learn software delivery flow
docs.text:
Before Jenkins syntax, understand the delivery chain:

  • developer writes code

  • code stored in Git

  • pipeline triggered

  • dependencies resolved

  • code compiled/test executed

  • artifact built

  • artifact scanned

  • artifact versioned

  • artifact pushed to registry/repository

  • deployment triggered

  • rollout validated

  • rollback possible

docs.title: 8.2 CI/CD core concepts
docs.text:
You need clean understanding of:

  • CI vs CD

  • pipeline stages

  • runners/agents

  • artifacts

  • environment promotion

  • secrets in pipelines

  • idempotent deployment

  • immutable artifacts

  • canary vs blue-green vs rolling deployment

  • approvals and gates

  • test pyramid basics

  • release management

  • pipeline observability

docs.title: 8.3 Jenkins fundamentals
docs.text:
Then go tool-specific:

  • controller vs agent

  • freestyle vs pipeline

  • declarative vs scripted

  • Jenkinsfile

  • shared libraries

  • credentials handling

  • agent labels

  • workspace management

  • parallel stages

  • post actions

  • retry and timeout handling

  • failure reporting

You should be able to explain:

  • Why pipelines fail intermittently

  • Why shared libraries reduce pipeline duplication

  • Why keeping business logic in Jenkinsfiles becomes messy

  • How to design secure pipeline credential usage

docs.title: 9. Phase 6 — Cloud fundamentals with AWS
docs.text:
Your CV mentions AWS, EKS, ECR, IAM Identity Center, ALB/NLB, EC2, VPC.

Do not start AWS from services list. Start from cloud operating model.

docs.title: 9.1 Learn the cloud mindset
docs.text:
Understand:

  • shared responsibility model

  • elasticity

  • pay-as-you-go

  • regional design

  • managed vs self-managed services

  • identity-first access control

  • fault isolation

  • automation-first provisioning

docs.title: 9.2 Must-master AWS foundations
docs.text:
Start with:

  • regions and AZs

  • accounts

  • VPC

  • subnets

  • route tables

  • internet gateway

  • NAT gateway

  • security groups

  • NACLs

  • EC2

  • AMIs

  • EBS

  • ELB basics

  • IAM users, roles, policies

  • CloudWatch basics

  • S3 fundamentals

  • DNS with Route 53

  • Systems Manager basics

Then move to:

  • ECR

  • EKS

  • IAM Identity Center

  • autoscaling concepts

  • KMS

  • Secrets Manager / Parameter Store

You should be able to answer:

  • Why use private subnets?

  • SG vs NACL?

  • Why use IAM role instead of static keys?

  • ALB vs NLB?

  • EBS vs S3?

  • Why multi-AZ matters?

docs.title: 10. Phase 7 — Infrastructure as Code and configuration management
docs.text: Your CV includes Terraform, Ansible, PowerCLI, GitOps workflows, provisioning automation.

docs.title: 10.1 Terraform from true basics
docs.text:
Terraform is not “write .tf files.” Terraform is a desired-state engine.

You need to understand:

  • desired state

  • providers

  • resources

  • data sources

  • variables

  • outputs

  • state

  • backend

  • modules

  • dependency graph

  • plan vs apply

  • drift

  • lifecycle

  • import

  • remote state

  • workspaces

Senior-level Terraform maturity means you can answer:

  • What is in terraform state and why is it sensitive?

  • Why does drift happen?

  • When should a module be split?

  • Why can badly designed modules become an anti-pattern?

  • Why is terraform apply from local laptop risky in enterprise environments?

docs.title: 10.2 Ansible from fundamentals
docs.text:
Ansible concepts:

  • inventory

  • playbook

  • task

  • role

  • variable precedence

  • idempotency

  • handlers

  • facts

  • templates

  • modules

  • vault

  • tags

  • conditionals

You must understand:

  • why idempotency matters

  • when to use shell vs module

  • role structure

  • inventory modeling

  • secrets management

  • Jinja2 basics

docs.title: 10.3 GitOps thinking
docs.text:
GitOps is not “store YAML in Git.”

It means:

  • Git is source of truth

  • changes are auditable

  • desired state is versioned

  • automated reconciliation happens

  • rollback is version-based

  • drift becomes visible

docs.title: 11. Phase 8 — Containers and Kubernetes
docs.text: This is one of the biggest areas in your CV: kubeadm, Cluster API, Helm, RBAC, Ingress, Longhorn, Ceph, service accounts, Vault, EKS.

docs.title: 11.1 Containers before Kubernetes
docs.text:
Learn:

  • what a container is

  • namespaces and cgroups at conceptual level

  • image vs container

  • Dockerfile basics

  • layers

  • image registries

  • entrypoint vs command

  • bind mounts vs volumes

  • environment variables

  • networking basics

  • why containers are not VMs

You should understand why:

  • image size matters

  • root user in container is risky

  • mutable containers are bad practice

  • tagging strategy matters

docs.title: 11.2 Kubernetes fundamentals
docs.text:
Do not jump into cluster deployment first.

Master these objects:

  • Pod

  • Deployment

  • ReplicaSet

  • StatefulSet

  • DaemonSet

  • Service

  • ConfigMap

  • Secret

  • Namespace

  • Ingress

  • PV/PVC

  • StorageClass

  • ServiceAccount

  • Role / ClusterRole

  • RoleBinding / ClusterRoleBinding

Then cluster concepts:

  • control plane

  • etcd

  • scheduler

  • kubelet

  • kube-proxy

  • CNI

  • CSI

  • admission control

  • reconciliation loop

You should be able to explain:

  • why pod is ephemeral

  • why service is needed

  • when to use StatefulSet

  • what happens when node goes NotReady

  • why liveness and readiness are different

  • why requests and limits matter

  • why resource pressure leads to eviction

docs.title: 11.3 Kubernetes operations maturity
docs.text:
Then move to:

  • kubeadm architecture

  • Cluster API concepts

  • cluster bootstrap

  • worker join process

  • upgrades

  • certificate rotation

  • ingress controller

  • Helm packaging

  • storage integration

  • secrets handling

  • Vault integration

  • backup/restore of etcd

  • troubleshooting CrashLoopBackOff, ImagePullBackOff, Pending pods

  • metrics-server basics

  • RBAC troubleshooting

docs.title: 12. Phase 9 — Observability and SRE foundations
docs.text:
Your CV has Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, ELK, Nagios, SNMP, Wazuh, CrowdSec, certificate monitoring, traffic flow visibility.

You need to build this in layers.

docs.title: 12.1 What observability really means
docs.text:
Three core pillars:

  • metrics

  • logs

  • traces

Plus practical ops pillars:

  • alerting

  • dashboards

  • runbooks

  • SLOs/SLIs

  • incident workflow

docs.title: 12.2 Metrics foundation
docs.text:
Learn:

  • counters

  • gauges

  • histograms

  • summaries

  • scraping model

  • exporters

  • label cardinality

  • PromQL basics

  • alert rules

  • recording rules

You must understand:

  • why high cardinality is dangerous

  • why “CPU high” alert alone is weak

  • why alert fatigue happens

  • why symptoms and causes should be separated

docs.title: 12.3 Logging foundation
docs.text:
Learn:

  • structured vs unstructured logs

  • log levels

  • parsing

  • indexing cost

  • retention

  • correlation

  • centralization

  • noise reduction

docs.title: 12.4 SRE basics
docs.text:
Understand:

  • SLA

  • SLI

  • SLO

  • error budget

  • toil

  • MTTD, MTTR

  • incident severity

  • postmortem culture

  • reliability vs release velocity tradeoff

docs.title: 13. Phase 10 — Security engineering fundamentals
docs.text:
Your CV includes RBAC, CIS hardening, Vault, IAM Identity Center, segmentation, certificate monitoring, Wazuh, CrowdSec.

This should be learned as control layers.

docs.title: 13.1 First principles
docs.text:
Understand:

  • CIA triad

  • least privilege

  • zero trust

  • defense in depth

  • identity over IP-based trust

  • secrets lifecycle

  • patching

  • hardening

  • auditability

docs.title: 13.2 Core security domains for DevOps
docs.text:
Learn:

  • Linux hardening

  • CIS concepts

  • SSH security

  • certificates and TLS basics

  • RBAC in Kubernetes

  • IAM in AWS

  • secrets management

  • network segmentation

  • image scanning

  • vulnerability management

  • logging and detection

  • change control

You should be able to answer:

  • Why hardcoding secrets is bad even in private repos

  • Why Kubernetes secret is not full secret management

  • Why IAM wildcards create future risk

  • Why segmentation contains blast radius

docs.title: 14. Phase 11 — Databases, backup, and recovery basics
docs.text:
Your CV mentions MSSQL, MySQL, PostgreSQL, corrupted DB recovery, backup automation, encryption, resilience.

You do not need to become DBA-first, but you must know:

  • relational DB basics

  • transactions

  • indexing basics

  • replication basics

  • consistency basics

  • backups: full, differential, incremental, transaction logs

  • point-in-time recovery

  • restore testing

  • corruption basics

  • performance basics

  • connection pooling basics

Senior-level practical truth: A backup is only trusted after a successful restore test.

docs.title: 15. Phase 12 — Disaster recovery and architecture thinking
docs.text:
Your CV strongly positions you in DR, BC, DC-DR, capacity planning, HA, failover, recovery execution.

This is senior-level territory.

You need to understand:

  • HA vs DR

  • RTO vs RPO

  • active-active vs active-passive

  • backup vs replication

  • failover vs switchover

  • dependency mapping

  • application recovery sequence

  • DNS failover

  • database recovery order

  • capacity headroom

  • runbooks

  • DR drills

  • business criticality tiers

You should be able to design:

  • infra redundancy

  • app redundancy

  • recovery orchestration

  • validation and rollback during DR

  • communications path during incident

docs.title: 16. What “senior DevOps engineer” actually means
docs.text: To become senior, you need growth in 5 dimensions:

docs.title: 16.1 Technical depth
docs.text: You can troubleshoot down to packet flow, process state, configuration drift, and control-plane failure.

docs.title: 16.2 System design
docs.text: You can build solutions with clear failure boundaries, automation flows, security controls, and operational ownership.

docs.title: 16.3 Operational maturity
docs.text: You think in monitoring, backups, rollbacks, audits, capacity, and incident response.

docs.title: 16.4 Decision-making
docs.text: You know when not to overengineer.

docs.title: 16.5 Mentoring
docs.text: You can explain a complex system simply to juniors and standardize team workflows.

docs.title: 17. Your structured learning path from internship level
docs.text: Here is the sequence I recommend for you.

docs.title: Stage 1 — Apprentice foundation
docs.text:
Duration focus: build depth, not speed

Learn in order:

  1. Linux

  2. Networking

  3. Git

  4. Bash/Python basics

  5. Virtualization basics

  6. Storage basics

Goal: You can deploy and troubleshoot a server end to end.

docs.title: Stage 2 — Junior DevOps foundation
docs.text:
Learn:

  1. CI/CD fundamentals

  2. AWS fundamentals

  3. Terraform

  4. Ansible

  5. container basics

  6. monitoring basics

Goal: You can automate infrastructure and delivery safely.

docs.title: Stage 3 — Mid-level platform engineer
docs.text:
Learn:

  1. Kubernetes architecture

  2. Helm

  3. ingress and storage

  4. observability stack

  5. security and IAM

  6. GitOps patterns

Goal: You can run a production platform, not just deploy apps.

docs.title: Stage 4 — Senior DevOps / platform maturity
docs.text:
Learn:

  1. multi-environment architecture

  2. HA/DR design

  3. capacity planning

  4. incident leadership

  5. compliance and hardening

  6. standardization and reusable platform patterns

Goal: You can own platform outcomes across people, systems, and risk.

docs.title: 18. Weekly study structure I would use to mentor you
docs.text: For every topic, use this method:

docs.title: Layer A — Concept
docs.text: What it is, why it exists, what problem it solves.

docs.title: Layer B — Internal mechanics
docs.text: How it works under the hood.

docs.title: 19. What you should not do
docs.text:
Avoid these mistakes:

  • memorizing commands without understanding flow

  • learning Kubernetes before Linux and networking

  • learning Terraform before understanding infrastructure

  • copying CI/CD pipelines without knowing stage logic

  • learning cloud only from certification notes

  • relying on tutorials instead of building labs

  • focusing only on deployment, not operations

  • ignoring backup, logging, and rollback design

  • writing metrics on CV that you cannot defend in detail

docs.title: 20. How I suggest we do this together
docs.text:
We should turn your CV into a full mentoring syllabus and cover every domain one by one from zero to senior level.

The best sequence for you is:

Module 1: Linux fundamentals Module 2: Networking fundamentals Module 3: Virtualization and storage Module 4: Git, Bash, Python, Go foundations Module 5: CI/CD and Jenkins Module 6: AWS core services and architecture Module 7: Terraform and Ansible Module 8: Docker and container fundamentals Module 9: Kubernetes from zero to production Module 10: Observability and SRE Module 11: Security, IAM, Vault, hardening Module 12: DR, HA, architecture, and senior engineering mindset

docs.title: 21. My recommendation for the first lesson
docs.text:
We should start with:

Linux + Networking foundations

Because if these two are weak, everything else becomes memorization instead of engineering.

In the next step, I can build you a complete week-by-week mentoring roadmap for all 12 modules, with:

That will turn your CV into a real execution plan.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2026-03-22 Updated Date: Sunday 22nd March 2026 13:43:06 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

DevOps is the combination of sets of practices, philosophies, and tools that would increase the organizations ability to deliver applications and services faster. This sets of practice includes using traditional software development and infrastructure management process.

DevOps is distinguished mainly because of Continuous Integration and Continuously delivering products which support faster updates and development changes even for the minor changes. With the help of CI/CD pipelines sets of rules are defined which would govern all the Software Development Lifecycle process.

docs.title: Phase 1: Plan (Dev)
docs.text: Before any project is started, the team would define what they are creating. Here, they define Goal, Key Activities, Tools Tools:* Jira, Click up

docs.title: Phase 2: Code (Dev)
docs.text: After the initial planning stage, developers would write code and publish it to the git repository whether it be deployed on the on-premise or cloud. Tools: GitHub, Gitlab, Bitbucket

docs.title: Phase 3: Build (Dev)
docs.text: Once the code is committed to the Version Control repository, it is pulled to the environment where it is build/compiled into an executable format. Tools: Maven, Gradle, Docker

docs.title: Phase 4: Test (Dev)
docs.text: Based on the codes pulled and the compiled package is generated. It would be tested either via the unit tests written on the code itself or smoke tests are done. So that no any bugs would be reported while deploying on the production environment. Tools: Selenium, Junit, SonarQube

docs.title: Phase 5: Release (Ops)
docs.text: Once all the tests are passed then it is ready to be deployed on the production environment. So, now which version is to be released on the production environment is looked over and approval for the deployment version is taken care of. Tools: Jenkins, GitLab CI.

docs.title: Phase 6: Deploy (Ops)
docs.text: Once received the versioning details and appropriate approval the product is then ready to be deployed on the production environment. So, that user would not face any issues while running the application/services. Tools: Terraform, Ansible, Kubernetes, Docker

docs.title: Phase 7: Operate (Ops)
docs.text: Once the code is live, then this phase involves the day to day management of the infrastructure hosting the application is to be managed. Tools: Ansible, PowerShell, Bash, Chef, Puppet

docs.title: Phase 8: Monitor (Ops)
docs.text: The data collected from the live application is analyzed, user behavior is understood then feedback is sent to the team for the optimization of the application. Which is now moves back to phase 1 (Plan stage) Tools: Prometheus, Grafana, ELK, Nagios

docs.title: 7Cs of DevOps
docs.text:
1. Continuous Development
  2. Continuous Integration
  3. Continuous Testing
  4. Continuous Deployment/Continuous Delivery
  5. Continuous Monitoring
  6. Continuous Feedback
  7. Continuous Operations

docs.text:
📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2026-03-28 Updated Date: Saturday 28th March 2026 15:35:14 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: Q1. You see a production server with 100% CPU. Walk through your diagnostic approach.
docs.text:
Answer: Start broad, narrow down, then act. Never guess and restart — always understand the root cause first. Bash

# Step 1: Immediate overview
top -b -n1 | head -20          # snapshot — which PID is consuming CPU?
uptime                          # load average trend (1m, 5m, 15m)

# Step 2: Identify the offending process
top -b -n1 -o %CPU | head -15
pidstat -u 2 5                  # per-process CPU, 5 samples at 2s intervals

# Step 3: What is that process doing?
strace -p <PID> -c -T 2>&1 | head -20   # syscall profile
perf top -p <PID>                         # kernel-level hot functions (need perf)
ls -la /proc/<PID>/fd | wc -l            # file descriptor count

# Step 4: Is it CPU-bound or I/O wait?
vmstat 1 5
# If wa (iowait) > 20%: storage problem, not pure CPU
# If us (user) near 100%: application code
# If sy (system/kernel) elevated: kernel issue, syscall storm

# Step 5: Memory pressure contributing?
free -h
cat /proc/meminfo | grep -E 'MemFree|Cached|SwapUsed|Dirty'

# Step 6: If Java/.NET process — get thread dump
jstack <PID> > /tmp/threaddump.txt
kill -3 <PID>                   # also triggers thread dump to stdout

# Step 7: Network causing CPU load?
ss -s                           # socket summary
cat /proc/net/softnet_stat      # NIC softirq drops

docs.title: VMStat Command Details
docs.text:
Mnemonic:

“Run Block - Swap Free BuffCache - SwapIn SwapOut - BlockIn BlockOut - Interrupt Context - User System Idle Wait Steal”

Field Meaning Mnemonic r running queue Run b blocked (I/O wait) Block

Signals

r > CPU cores high CPU contention b > 0 -> I/O bottleneck

docs.title: Disk
docs.text:
Field Meaning Mnemonic bi blocks in (read) Block In bo blocks out (write) Block Out

Signals

High bi/bo + wa → disk bottleneck Sudden spikes → burst workload or flush

Field Meaning Mnemonic us user CPU User sy kernel CPU System id idle Idle wa I/O wait Wait st stolen (VM) Steal

docs.title: 🟥 CPU Bottleneck
docs.text:
👉 “Run high, Idle low”

r ↑, id ↓

docs.title: 🟥 Memory Bottleneck
docs.text:
👉 “Swap is death”

si/so > 0

docs.title: 🟥 Disk Bottleneck
docs.text:
👉 “Blocked + Waiting”

b > 0, wa ↑

docs.title: 🟥 Thread Contention
docs.text:
👉 “Context explosion”

cs ↑↑

docs.title: 🟥 Virtualization Issue
docs.text:
👉 “Steal means host stealing CPU”

st > 0

docs.title: Step 1 — Detect
docs.text:
Bash
vmstat 1

👉 “Something is wrong: memory/disk/cpu”

docs.title: Step 2 — Narrow Down
docs.text:
If disk suspected: Bash

iostat -x 1

If CPU: Bash

top

If memory:

Bash
free -m
