docs.text:
• Identify process
  • Identify pattern
  • Identify spike source

docs.title: Fundamentals
docs.text:
📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2026-03-22 Updated Date: Sunday 22nd March 2026 14:21:04 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

The basic set of commands or the mindset that you must have for the embedded into you muscle memory, so that it would be useful in case of any issues or troubleshooting the issues or concerns raised on any of the environment.

docs.title: Boot Process
docs.text: The Boot Process on Linux - On-premise and EC2 - AWS are kind of similar. Apart from few components are added on the Cloud based instance creation. The EC2 Boot process only includes the additional step that would be carried out on the cloud infrastructure. Like for the On-premise environment if we create the VM we define the resources first on the VMware or any of the virtualization layer.

docs.title: 🖥️EC2 Boot Process - Pre-provisioning
docs.text:
When we first launch an EC2 instance AWS would process these information:

  1. AMI ID: The AMI has the type of the Operating system that is to be deployed. This AMI ID also determines what type of boot mode is to be used. UEFI/BIOS E.g.. RHEL, Ubuntu etc.
  2. Instance Type: This looks which resource to apply to the Instance that is created. like t2micro, t3nano etc.
  3. Subnet: What subnet is to be applied while creating an ec2 instance and where the subnet lies on which VPC
  4. Security Groups: What policies are to be applied, if needed to create the new policy then it would create the policy first.
  5. Key Pair: For the accessibility of the ec2 instance what public key is to be used.
  6. IAM Instance Profile: This verifies the role assigned to the EC2 instance that would be created. Suppose if the EC2 instance needs to have the access of the s3 bucket or the dynamo DB, then IAM Instance Profile would be attached which would be the separate container or the service provided by AWS which would attach the only one policy defined in the IAM role. This only acts as a wrapper as EC2 API could not directly attach role.
  7. Block Device Mapping: This defines how the storage is attached to the instance at the launch time. We are basically telling AWS where the disk exists and where should they come from and how they should behave. The volumes are attached to the instances via Nitro and exposed to the instance as NVME devices.
  8. Meta Data Options:
  9. User Data: This stores the first time configuration script that is to be executed inside the instance. This would be used later on at the cloud-init step.

docs.title: ⚙️ Boot Process
docs.text:
On the EC2 once the pre-provisioning tasks are completed then once the instance or VM is created then it would be powered on this process.

  1. BIOS/UEFI: framework is loaded which stores the details of the partition type that is to be used either GPT or MBR.
  2. POST: after the BIOS is loaded then it would check all the external devices like keyboard, mouse, CPU, RAM, Disks would be checked and if any corruption is seen then it would be shown on the console itself.
  3. Boot Loader: Grub2 bootloader would be loaded from /boot/grub2/grub.cfg which would then trigger the kernel modules that would be required for the OS to boot. This presents the boot menu, and loads the selected kernel image (vmlinuz) and initial RAM disk (initramfs) into the memory.
  4. Kernel Load: Once received the signal for the modules to be loaded then all the kernel dependent modules are loaded and once all the modules are loaded then it would send the signal to the init or systemd initialization. Initramfs would be mounted temporary root filesystem. Once mounted, kernes executes binary in /sbin/init which in latest linux is systemd (PID 1).
  5. Init/Systemd: Systemd services would be started which would start all the services required for the system services.
  6. Run Level: At this stage OS would be started and then other user level services would be started at this stage like: user login, graphical interface, network, user profiles initialization, cloud init initializations with user data scripts, configurations inside /etc

docs.title: Misconfigured /etc/fstab
docs.text:
If the ec2 instance is misconfigured on /etc/fstab then we could use the disk mounted on ec2 instance inside / partition, we could mount to another ec2 instance and use that instance to fix the issue.

Steps: 1. Navigate to the ec2 instance which is corrupted. (checks would fail on the instance) 2. From instance state get the screenshot of the current state inside the ec2 instance which would give more insight of the root cause of the issue. 3. Power off the corrupted instance 4. Inside the storage detach the EBS storage which is mounted on the / partition. 5. Create new instance and attach the corrupted detached disk as new disk inside new ec2 instance 6. Inside the new ec2 instance new disk is seen. 7. Using file command to know which partition is the old / partition. file -s /dev/nvme2n1p1 8. Mount this to any of the temporary file and patch the issue in this directory. mount -t xfs -o nouuid /dev/nvme2n1p1 /mnt 9. Update the /etc/fstab and fix the corrupted entry. 10. Reattach the storage to the old ec2 instance and try powering up the instance.

docs.title: Misconfigured /etc/fstab
docs.text: Attach the live iso to the seperate disk in the VM then after follow the same process as that of the ec2 instance to fix any of the issues whether it be as normal as /etc/fstab or updating the sysctl configurations.

docs.title: Stuck in boot:
docs.text:
Edit the grub config during the boot append the line at the end to boot into rescue mode. systemd.unit=rescue.target Once you would be booted to the rescue mode you could use the commands below to dig down the issues in the boot process and why is issues is raised.

Command Result Obtained systemd-analyze Total boot time systemd-analyze critical-chain Check systemd unit startup ordering and critical path systemd-analyze plot > boot.svg Visual timeline of the boot systemd-analyze blame Show boot time breakdown per-unit breakdown journalctl -xb Current boot logs with explanations journalctl -b -1 --priority=err View boot logs from previous failed boot journalctl --list-boots List all recorded boots

Rebuild Initramfs if corrupted

dracut --force /boo/initramfs.$(uname -r).img $(uname -r)

Best Practise

Always keep at least one known-good kernel entry in GRUB. Use gruby --default-kernel to verify the default kernel before rebooting other updates.

Pitfall

Editing /boot/grub2/grub.cfg directly instead of using grub2-mkconfig. Direct edits are overwritten on kernel updates.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
Directory Used For /etc Storing the configurations of the services installed. /var Storing the logs of the services or the system. /home Home directory of the new users created /usr Storing the user's application, libraries, documentations and binaries for all users /bin Store the executable for the basic system operations /opt Installing the additional custom applications /tmp World writeable directory created by the systems and application. Would clear the files inside this directory after reboot.

In cloud environment the logs that are generated inside /var/log are shipped to CloudWatch Logs

docs.title: Users, Groups, Permissions
docs.text:
Most general commands used for the user and group manipulation in Linux are: chmod chown usermod useradd groupadd For creating a system user whose id is less than 1000, we could: usermod -r -s /bin/bash birat This would create a system user birat like that of mysql, nginx, tomcat, wildfly, docker, kubeadm and such.

Linux AWS User IAM User Group IAM Group Permission IAM Policy Root AWS root

In AWS IAM are collections of individual users used to simplify the permission management by attaching policies which is created in json format by defining the allowed actions (e.g. read-only, admin) to the group rather than individual users. Users could belong to the multiple group, and would inherit all the permissions.

E.g.: AM policy if the user belongs to multiple groups like: One group is admin with full access to aws resources across all regions and another group has read only access to limited resources like: ec2, s3 in us-east1 then the user would have full admin privilege across all region

E.g: Only Use explicit deny for the guardrails only. like: 1. Restrict regions change and deploy/build resources across other regions. 2. Prevent deletion of the resources

docs.title: IAM Decision Order
docs.text: Explicit Deny -> Highest priority Explicit Allow -> Evaluated only if no deny Default Deny -> Fallback

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. A single running instance of a program
  2. Uses memory space and seperate cgroups and namespace
  3. Has file Decriptors

docs.title: Threads
docs.text:
1. Its a light weight execution of a process.
  2. Single process would have multiple threads
  3. They share the same namespace as that of process
  4. They share the memory with other threads spawned from the process

docs.title: Signals
docs.text:
1. Its a notification mechanism that is sent to a process
  2. They tell the process about shutting down the process.

docs.title: Notifications and its meanings:
docs.text: Signal Meaning SIGTERM fracefull shutdown SIGKILL force kill SIGHUP reload configurations

docs.title: Key Differences
docs.text: Feature Process Thread Signal Type Execution unit Sub-unit Control mechanism Memory Isolated Shared No memory Communication IPC shared memory async event Creation cost High Low Very low Example nginx worker thread kill command

docs.title: Systemd
docs.text: Its the service and system management software which would initialize the system, manages services and control the system resources from startup time and runtime. Systemd replaces the init system with new systemd as it is faster than init.d. This is the first process that is started in the system with PID 1

docs.title: Commands and Details of Systemd
docs.text: Command Used for systemctl --version Checking version of systemd systemctl list-units List all the systemd services loaded and running on the system systemctl isolate graphical.target Switch to the graphical interface. Could use same concept to troubleshoot any stage systemctl edit servicename Edit the systemd service file. systemctl daemon-reload To refresh the configuration that is changed on the service file. systemctl list-units --type=timer Get all the timers and its associated schedules and services systemctl start/stop/status servicename To get the status of the service that is running on the system. journalctl -xeu servicename To view the logs of the service that ran journalctl -u servicename --since "since 30 minutes ago" shows logs of the systemd service of last 30 minutes. journalctl -u servicename -p err --since "since n minutes ago" Shows only error message of the service of last n minutes

docs.title: Systemd Service inside AWS
docs.text: Service Name Used for cloud-init Running user data and assigning IP to the new instance. Configure instance on first boot. amazon-ssm-agent automation and patching the aws instance incase of any issues. Its equivalent to vmware tools but for AWS. It does not use SSH access. amazon-cloudwatch-agent This service sends the logs on /var/logs to CloudWatch

docs.title: Packages
docs.text:
Its the compiled binaries in Linux which would perform the sets of tasks. Suppose if you want to know if server is reachable or not, then you could use the ping command. Then this ping command is the package. Or, if you want to browse the internet then you would need browser so the Firefox, brave, google chrome is the package.

OS package manager Red Hat yum/ dnf Debian apt Arch pacman OpenSuse zypper Alpine apk Command Example Purpose yum install yum install -y ip-utils Install the specific packages like ping yum remove yum remove -y ping remove the installed packages yum search yum search ping Search for the package based on name yum info yum info ping Lists description of the package yum update yum update -y nginx update the specific package or just yum update -y for updating entire packages installed. yum repolist yum repolist List all the repositories set up on the system. yum history yum history Give the history of the past operations on yum packages. yum history undo <ID> yum history undo 2 this would redo the transactions. yum localinstall <rpm package> yum localinstall nginx.rpm This would install the nginx package that is downloaded to the machine. rpm -qa rpm -qa This would list all the packages installed on the system.

docs.title: Repositories
docs.text:
Repositories are the sets of urls which would have the information of the packages that is to be installed. The configuration file for yum and related utilities is located at /etc/yum.conf and for defining the individual files for the downloading the packages the repositories could be configured in /etc/yum.repos.d/ directory.

If working on air-gapped environment or faster installation of the packages we could use the offline method by directly mounting the iso files to download the packages by adding local repositories. Its ideal for datacenters, productions, labs, servers. This provides the centralized package management.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
Provide a centralized package source on ServerA for offline RHEL 8/9 clients (ServerB, ServerC, etc.) using:

  • rhel-iso/ for BaseOS and AppStream
  • custom/ for manually uploaded RPMs
  • epel/ optionally, only if you separately obtain real EPEL package content

This supports normal yum install / dnf install on clients with no direct internet access, as long as packages and metadata are present on ServerA. (Red Hat Documentation)

docs.title: 2. Scope
docs.text:
Applies to:

  • RHEL 8 / RHEL 9
  • Rocky Linux 8/9
  • AlmaLinux 8/9

This SOP assumes:

  • ServerA hosts packages over HTTP
  • ServerB consumes those repositories
  • Binary DVD ISO is available for the same major version and architecture as the clients

docs.title: 3. Architecture
docs.text:
Repository server layout on ServerA:

Text Only
/var/www/html/repos/
├── rhel-iso/          # copied or bind-mounted ISO content
├── custom/            # uploaded RPMs + repodata
└── epel/              # optional extra repo content

Client access pattern on ServerB:

  • http://<ServerA>/repos/rhel-iso/BaseOS
  • http://<ServerA>/repos/rhel-iso/AppStream
  • http://<ServerA>/repos/custom

docs.title: 4.1 Platform prerequisites
docs.text:
• ServerA installed and reachable from clients over HTTP
  • ServerA and clients on matching OS major version and architecture
  • RHEL 8/9 Binary DVD ISO available
  • Sufficient disk space for ISO copy and custom RPMs
  • Root or sudo access on ServerA and clients

docs.title: 4.2 Package prerequisites on ServerA
docs.text:
Install these on ServerA:

Bash
dnf install -y httpd createrepo_c rsync

createrepo_c is required to generate repository metadata for the custom repo. Apache/httpd is used to publish content over HTTP. Creating a Yum/DNF repository requires metadata generation in the RPM directory. (Red Hat Documentation)

docs.title: 4.3 Network prerequisites
docs.text:
From ServerB to ServerA:

  • TCP/80 reachable
  • name resolution or static IP available

docs.title: 4.4 Security prerequisites
docs.text:
Decide whether you will:

  • bootstrap with gpgcheck=0, or
  • later implement signed repositories

This SOP uses gpgcheck=0 for offline bootstrap simplicity.

docs.title: 5.1 Create repository directories on ServerA
5.2 Mount the Binary DVD ISO on ServerA
docs.text:
If using an ISO file:

Bash
mount -o loop /path/to/rhel-9.x-x86_64-dvd.iso /mnt/rhel-iso

If using attached virtual media:

Bash
mount /dev/sr0 /mnt/rhel-iso

Validate expected repository trees:

Bash
ls -1 /mnt/rhel-iso

You should see content such as BaseOS, AppStream, and media.repo. RHEL installation media contains BaseOS and AppStream content used during installation. (Red Hat Documentation)

docs.title: Preferred method: copy ISO contents
docs.text:
Bash
rsync -aH /mnt/rhel-iso/ /var/www/html/repos/rhel-iso/

This is operationally simpler than relying on persistent bind mounts.

docs.title: Alternative method: bind mount
docs.text:
Bash
mount --bind /mnt/rhel-iso /var/www/html/repos/rhel-iso

If you use bind mount, persist it in /etc/fstab after validation.

docs.title: 5.4 Initialize the custom repository
docs.text:
Create metadata even if the repo starts empty or nearly empty:

Bash
createrepo_c /var/www/html/repos/custom

A valid RPM repository requires generated repodata/. (Red Hat Documentation)

docs.title: 5.5 Start and enable Apache
docs.text:
Bash
systemctl enable --now httpd
systemctl status httpd --no-pager

docs.title: 5.6 Open firewall for HTTP
docs.text:
If firewalld is enabled:

Bash
firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=http
firewall-cmd --reload

docs.title: 5.7 Restore SELinux contexts
docs.text:
Bash
restorecon -Rv /var/www/html/repos

This ensures Apache can serve copied content under the standard web root.

docs.title: 6. Client Configuration on ServerB
docs.text:
Create /etc/yum.repos.d/internal-offline.repo:

INI
[internal-baseos]
name=Internal BaseOS
baseurl=http://192.168.25.15/repos/rhel-iso/BaseOS
enabled=1
gpgcheck=0

[internal-appstream]
name=Internal AppStream
baseurl=http://192.168.25.15/repos/rhel-iso/AppStream
enabled=1
gpgcheck=0

[internal-custom]
name=Internal Custom Repo
baseurl=http://192.168.25.15/repos/custom
enabled=1
gpgcheck=0

Then refresh metadata on the client:

Bash
yum clean all
yum makecache
yum repolist

BaseOS provides the core OS functionality; AppStream provides additional user-space applications and runtimes. Both are standard RHEL 8/9 repositories. (Red Hat Documentation)

docs.title: 7. Installing Packages from ServerB
docs.text:
Examples:

Bash
yum install -y vim
yum install -y tcpdump
yum install -y mtr

Behavior:

  • If a package exists in BaseOS or AppStream, ServerB downloads it from ServerA’s ISO-backed repo.
  • If a package exists only in custom/, ServerB downloads it from ServerA’s custom repo.
  • Dependencies are resolved from all enabled internal repos on ServerA. DNF/YUM resolves packages from enabled repositories using their metadata. (dnf-plugins-core.readthedocs.io)

docs.title: 8.1 Add extra RPMs to the custom repo on ServerA
docs.text:
Bash
cp /incoming/*.rpm /var/www/html/repos/custom/
createrepo_c --update /var/www/html/repos/custom
restorecon -Rv /var/www/html/repos/custom

createrepo_c --update refreshes metadata for an existing repository after adding packages. (Red Hat Documentation)

docs.title: 8.2 Refresh cache on clients
docs.text:
Bash
yum clean metadata
yum makecache

docs.title: 8.3 Install normally on clients
docs.text:
Bash
yum install -y <package>

docs.title: 9. Optional: EPEL Handling
docs.text:
Do not confuse epel-release with the actual EPEL repository content.

  • epel-release is mainly the package that drops a repo definition onto a host
  • it does not contain the full installable package set

Only use /var/www/html/repos/epel if you actually copied or mirrored EPEL package content there. For offline use, that content must be obtained separately.

docs.title: 10.1 Validate on ServerA
docs.text:
Check metadata endpoints:

Bash
curl -I http://127.0.0.1/repos/rhel-iso/BaseOS/repodata/repomd.xml
curl -I http://127.0.0.1/repos/rhel-iso/AppStream/repodata/repomd.xml
curl -I http://127.0.0.1/repos/custom/repodata/repomd.xml

Expected result:

  • HTTP 200

If using ServerA IP:

Bash
curl -I http://192.168.25.15/repos/rhel-iso/BaseOS/repodata/repomd.xml

docs.title: 10.2 Validate on ServerB
docs.text:
Bash
yum clean all
yum makecache
yum repolist
yum info bash
yum info vim
yum info mtr

Expected result:

  • internal repositories visible in yum repolist
  • package metadata returned by yum info
  • installs succeed without internet

docs.title: 10.3 Functional validation
docs.text:
Install one package from each source:

Bash
yum install -y bash
yum install -y vim
yum install -y <custom-package>

docs.title: 11.1 Rollback client repo change
docs.text:
If client installs fail after repo cutover:

  1. disable the custom repo file:
Bash
mv /etc/yum.repos.d/internal-offline.repo /etc/yum.repos.d/internal-offline.repo.disabled
  1. clean cache:
Bash
yum clean all
  1. re-enable prior repo definitions or restore backed-up .repo files

Recommended before change:

Bash
cp -a /etc/yum.repos.d /etc/yum.repos.d.backup.$(date +%F-%H%M%S)

docs.title: 11.2 Rollback custom repo content on ServerA
docs.text:
If newly added RPMs break resolution:

  1. remove the newly added RPMs
  2. rebuild metadata
Bash
rm -f /var/www/html/repos/custom/<bad-package-pattern>*.rpm
createrepo_c --update /var/www/html/repos/custom
restorecon -Rv /var/www/html/repos/custom

docs.title: 11.3 Rollback ISO publication
docs.text:
If copied ISO content is wrong or corrupted:

Bash
rm -rf /var/www/html/repos/rhel-iso/*
rsync -aH /mnt/rhel-iso/ /var/www/html/repos/rhel-iso/
restorecon -Rv /var/www/html/repos/rhel-iso

docs.title: 11.4 Service rollback
docs.text:
If Apache changes break publication:

Bash
systemctl restart httpd
journalctl -u httpd -n 100 --no-pager

If necessary, revert Apache config from backup.

docs.title: 12.1 Symptom: yum makecache fails on ServerB
docs.text:
Check:

Bash
curl -I http://192.168.25.15/repos/rhel-iso/BaseOS/repodata/repomd.xml
curl -I http://192.168.25.15/repos/custom/repodata/repomd.xml

Likely causes:

  • HTTP blocked by firewall
  • Apache not running
  • wrong baseurl
  • repodata/ missing in custom repo

A reposync copy with --download-metadata is directly usable, while a custom RPM directory needs createrepo_c. (dnf-plugins-core.readthedocs.io)

docs.title: 12.2 Symptom: package not found
docs.text:
Check:

Bash
yum repolist
yum list available | grep -i <package>

Likely causes:

  • package is not on the ISO
  • package was not copied to custom/
  • client is only pointed to BaseOS/AppStream, not custom

docs.title: 12.3 Symptom: dependency resolution failure
docs.text:
Likely causes:

  • missing dependency RPMs in custom/
  • required package exists in a repo not enabled on ServerB
  • mixed OS major versions
  • architecture mismatch

docs.title: 12.4 Symptom: No available modular metadata for modular package
docs.text:
This is a known issue when creating a local repository from a small set of modular packages. Red Hat documents that modular packages need modular metadata; modulesync is recommended for redistribution of modular content. (Red Hat Customer Portal)

Action:

  • use full ISO AppStream where possible
  • avoid cherry-picking modular RPMs into custom/
  • if redistributing modules, build repo content with dnf modulesync

docs.title: 12.5 Symptom: Apache serves 403/404
docs.text:
Check:

Bash
systemctl status httpd --no-pager
ls -ld /var/www/html/repos
ls -l /var/www/html/repos/rhel-iso/BaseOS/repodata/repomd.xml
getenforce
restorecon -Rv /var/www/html/repos

Likely causes:

  • SELinux context incorrect
  • file path wrong
  • Apache stopped
  • bind mount missing after reboot

docs.title: 12.6 Symptom: wrong packages or dependency conflicts
docs.text:
Check:

Bash
cat /etc/redhat-release
uname -m
yum repolist -v

Likely causes:

  • ServerA and ServerB are on different major versions
  • x86_64 vs aarch64 mismatch
  • stale metadata on client

docs.title: 13.1 Change control
docs.text:
Before changing repos on ServerA:

  • back up client .repo definitions
  • log package additions to custom/
  • keep a manifest of uploaded RPMs

docs.title: 13.2 Recommended manifest file
docs.text:
On ServerA:

Bash
find /var/www/html/repos/custom -maxdepth 1 -name "*.rpm" -printf "%f\n" | sort > /var/www/html/repos/custom/PACKAGE_MANIFEST.txt

docs.title: 13.3 Patch cadence
docs.text:
• ISO-backed BaseOS/AppStream is static until you replace the ISO
  • custom/ changes whenever you upload new RPMs
  • document every refresh of createrepo_c --update

docs.title: 13.4 Snapshot strategy
docs.text:
Before major changes:

Bash
cp -a /var/www/html/repos/custom /var/www/html/repos/custom.backup.$(date +%F-%H%M%S)

If the filesystem supports hardlinks/snapshots, use them to reduce storage overhead. Red Hat notes that frozen repository copies can be maintained and deduplicated with hardlinks on the same filesystem. (Red Hat Customer Portal)

docs.title: 14.1 ServerA build steps
docs.text:
Bash
dnf install -y httpd createrepo_c rsync

mkdir -p /var/www/html/repos/{rhel-iso,custom,epel}
mkdir -p /mnt/rhel-iso

mount -o loop /path/to/rhel-9.x-x86_64-dvd.iso /mnt/rhel-iso
rsync -aH /mnt/rhel-iso/ /var/www/html/repos/rhel-iso/

createrepo_c /var/www/html/repos/custom

restorecon -Rv /var/www/html/repos

systemctl enable --now httpd

firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=http
firewall-cmd --reload

curl -I http://127.0.0.1/repos/rhel-iso/BaseOS/repodata/repomd.xml
curl -I http://127.0.0.1/repos/rhel-iso/AppStream/repodata/repomd.xml
curl -I http://127.0.0.1/repos/custom/repodata/repomd.xml

docs.title: 14.2 ServerB repo definition
docs.text:
INI
[internal-baseos]
name=Internal BaseOS
baseurl=http://192.168.25.15/repos/rhel-iso/BaseOS
enabled=1
gpgcheck=0

[internal-appstream]
name=Internal AppStream
baseurl=http://192.168.25.15/repos/rhel-iso/AppStream
enabled=1
gpgcheck=0

[internal-custom]
name=Internal Custom Repo
baseurl=http://192.168.25.15/repos/custom
enabled=1
gpgcheck=0

docs.title: 14.3 ServerB validation
docs.text:
Bash
yum clean all
yum makecache
yum repolist
yum info bash
yum info vim

docs.title: 14.4 Add custom RPMs later on ServerA
docs.text:
Bash
cp /incoming/*.rpm /var/www/html/repos/custom/
createrepo_c --update /var/www/html/repos/custom
restorecon -Rv /var/www/html/repos/custom

docs.title: 14.5 Refresh on ServerB
docs.text:
Bash
yum clean metadata
yum makecache
yum install -y <package>

docs.title: 15. Final Notes
docs.text:
For RHEL 8/9, this is the stable production pattern:

  • BaseOS/AppStream from Binary DVD ISO
  • custom/ for extra RPMs
  • Apache publication from ServerA
  • clients consume only internal HTTP repos

The only place you need extra caution is modular packages in AppStream. For those, prefer the full ISO AppStream repo or use modulesync rather than manually copying a handful of modular RPMs. (dnf-plugins-core.readthedocs.io)

If you want this turned into a Markdown file formatted for MkDocs/Obsidian, I can structure it with callouts, command blocks, and troubleshooting anchors.

docs.title: ResourceIsolation
docs.text: For Isolating the resources and process, Linux uses two kernel features cgroups and namespaces

docs.title: Cgroups & Namespaces
docs.text:
Control Groups (cgroups) and namespaces are the two Linux kernel features that make containers possible. Namespaces provide process isolation by creating separate views of system resources - PID namespace gives containers their own process tree (PID 1 inside container), network namespace provides isolated network stacks, mount namespace gives separate filesystem views, UTS namespace allows separate hostnames, and user namespace maps container UIDs to host UIDs for rootless containers.

Cgroups provide resource limiting and accounting. They organize processes into hierarchical groups and enforce limits on CPU time, memory usage, I/O bandwidth, and device access. Cgroups v2 (unified hierarchy) is now the default on modern kernels and uses a single hierarchy with all controllers. This is what containerd and Docker use under the hood - when you pass --memory=512m to docker run, it creates a cgroup with memory.max set to 536870912 bytes.

To limit a runaway process manually using cgroups v2, you create a new cgroup, set resource limits, and move the process into it. This is extremely useful for emergency situations where a process is consuming excessive resources but you cannot immediately kill it.

Bash
# Create a new cgroup
sudo mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/limited_group
# Set memory limit to 512MB
echo 536870912 | sudo tee /sys/fs/cgroup/limited_group/memory.max
# Set CPU limit to 50% of one core
echo '50000 100000' | sudo tee /sys/fs/cgroup/limited_group/cpu.max
# Move a runaway process (PID 12345) into the limited group
echo 12345 | sudo tee /sys/fs/cgroup/limited_group/cgroup.procs
# Verify
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/limited_group/cgroup.procs
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/limited_group/memory.current
# View namespace of a container process
ls -la /proc/<PID>/ns/
lsns                                   # List all namespaces
nsenter -t <PID> -n ip addr            # Enter network namespace

Best Practise

Use sytemd resource controls (MemoryMax=, CPUQuota=) for services rather than manually managing cgroups since systemd already manages cgroup hierarchy.

Pitfall

Mixing cgroups v1 and cgroups v2 controllers caused unpredictable behavious. Check Bash

mount|grep cgroup
to verify which version is active. Modern Kubernetes requires cgroups v2.

docs.title: Inspecting container cgroup limits:
docs.text:
Bash
# Find container cgroup path  
CONTAINER_ID=$(docker inspect --format '{{.Id}}' myapp)  
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/system.slice/docker-${CONTAINER_ID}.scope/memory.max  

# Check if a pod was OOMKilledkubectl describe pod <pod> | grep -A5 "Last State"  
# Look for: Reason: OOMKilled  

# Set kernel parameters for container workloads
sysctl -w vm.swappiness=10sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range="1024 65535"sysctl -w net.core.somaxconn=65535sysctl -w fs.inotify.max_user_watches=524288sysctl -w fs.inotify.max_user_instances=512

docs.title: Persistent kernel tuning for Kubernetes nodes (/etc/sysctl.d/99-k8s.conf):
docs.text:
Text Only
# Network performance  
net.core.somaxconn = 65535  
net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog = 8192  
net.core.netdev_max_backlog = 16384  
net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle = 0  
net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse = 1  

# Memory  
vm.swappiness = 1  
vm.overcommit_memory = 1  
kernel.panic = 10  
kernel.panic_on_oops = 1  

# inotify (critical for many k8s components)  
fs.inotify.max_user_watches = 524288  
fs.inotify.max_user_instances = 512  

# Conntrack for high-traffic nodes  
net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_max = 1048576  
net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_tcp_timeout_established = 86400

docs.text:
## 📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-06-18 Updated Date: Tuesday 17th June 2025 22:02:32 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 🛠 Dockerfile Best Practices
docs.text: Creating efficient, secure, and maintainable Dockerfiles is essential for performance and reproducibility. Below are recommended best practices:

docs.title: 1. Choose a Minimal Base Image
docs.text:
• Use official minimal images like alpine or debian-slim when possible.

  • Avoid bloated base images to reduce image size and vulnerabilities.

Docker
FROM node:20-alpine

docs.title: 2. Leverage Layer Caching
docs.text:
• Place commands that change least at the top of the Dockerfile.

  • Group RUN instructions to minimize image layers.

Docker
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y curl && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

docs.title: 3. Use .dockerignore
docs.text:
• Prevent unnecessary files from being copied into the image.
Text Only
node_modules
.git
*.log
Dockerfile

docs.title: 4. Avoid Installing Unnecessary Packages
docs.text:
• Keep your image clean and secure.

  • Use --no-install-recommends with apt.

Docker
RUN apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y python3

docs.title: 5. Use Multi-Stage Builds
docs.text:
• Compile or build in one stage, copy only what’s needed to final image.
Docker
FROM golang:1.20 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN go build -o main .

FROM alpine
COPY --from=builder /app/main /main
ENTRYPOINT ["/main"]

docs.title: 6. Set a Non-Root User
docs.text:
• Reduce security risk by not running containers as root.
Docker
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup
USER appuser

docs.title: 7. Use COPY Instead of ADD
docs.text: • COPY is more predictable. Use ADD only for auto-extracting archives or remote URLs.

docs.title: 8. Use Labels for Metadata
docs.text:
• Add maintainer, version, description, etc.
Docker
LABEL maintainer="yourname@example.com"
LABEL version="1.0"
LABEL description="My awesome app"

docs.title: 9. Specify Exact Version Numbers
docs.text:
• Helps avoid unexpected updates or changes.
Docker
RUN apt-get install -y nginx=1.18.*

docs.title: 10. Always Use CMD or ENTRYPOINT Correctly
docs.text:
• CMD is the default command.

  • ENTRYPOINT is useful for scripts and passing arguments.

Docker
CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]

docs.title: 🧩 Core Components of Docker
docs.text: Component Description Docker Engine The runtime environment for building and running containers. It includes the Docker daemon (dockerd), REST API, and CLI (docker). Images Immutable blueprints of containers. They include application code, dependencies, and the OS libraries. Built from Dockerfiles. Containers Running instances of Docker images. They are isolated from the host and other containers. Dockerfile A script containing instructions to build Docker images. It defines the base image, environment, commands, exposed ports, etc. Docker Compose A tool for defining and managing multi-container Docker applications using YAML configuration (docker-compose.yml). Volumes Docker’s mechanism for persistent storage. Data in volumes persists even after the container is deleted. Networks Allow communication between Docker containers and between containers and the outside world. Supports custom and predefined network drivers. Registry A storage and distribution system for Docker images. Public (Docker Hub) or private (Harbor, GitHub Container Registry, etc.).

docs.text:

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-06-18 Updated Date: Tuesday 17th June 2025 21:59:04 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 🛠 Running and Managing Docker Containers
docs.text: This document provides essential commands and practices to run, manage, and troubleshoot Docker containers effectively.

docs.title: Run a Container from an Image
docs.text:
Bash
docker run -d --name my-nginx -p 8080:80 nginx

  • -d: Detached mode (runs in background)

  • --name: Assigns a name to the container

  • -p: Maps port 8080 on host to port 80 in container

docs.title: Run with Interactive Terminal
docs.text:
Bash
docker run -it ubuntu /bin/bash
  • -it: Interactive terminal access

docs.title: Run and Remove After Exit
docs.text:
Bash
docker run --rm alpine echo "Hello, Docker!"

docs.title: List All Running Containers
docs.text:
Bash
docker ps

docs.title: List All Containers (including stopped)
docs.text:
Bash
docker ps -a

docs.title: Start, Stop, and Restart Containers
View Logs
docs.text:
Bash
docker logs <container_name>

docs.title: Execute Command Inside Container
docs.text:
Bash
docker exec -it <container_name> /bin/bash

docs.title: Remove a Container
docs.text:
Bash
docker rm <container_id_or_name>

docs.title: Remove All Stopped Containers
docs.text:
Bash
docker container prune

docs.title: Remove All Unused Resources
docs.text:
Bash
docker system prune -a

⚠️ Use with caution – this will remove unused containers, networks, volumes, and images.

docs.title: Inspect Container Details
docs.text:
Bash
docker inspect <container_name>

docs.title: Resource Usage (CPU, memory, etc.)
docs.text:
Bash
docker stats

docs.title: Network Inspection
📌 Tips
docs.text:
• Name containers meaningfully for easier management

  • Use docker-compose for multi-container apps

  • Monitor logs and resource usage for performance issues

  • Tag and version your images clearly

docs.title: Troubleshoot
docs.text:
## 📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-06-18 Updated Date: Wednesday 18th June 2025 10:54:31 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 🧪 Building and Debugging Docker Images
docs.text: This guide covers best practices, useful options, and techniques for building and debugging Docker images efficiently and effectively.

docs.title: Basic Build Command
docs.text:
Bash
docker build -t myapp:latest .

  • -t: Tags the image with a name and optionally a tag (default is latest)

  • .: Context path where the Dockerfile resides

docs.title: Specify Dockerfile Location
docs.text:
Bash
docker build -f path/to/Dockerfile .

docs.title: Add Build Arguments
docs.text:
Bash
docker build --build-arg VERSION=1.2.3 -t app:1.2.3 .
  • Use ARG in Dockerfile to access this value

docs.title: Build with No Cache
docs.text:
Bash
docker build --no-cache -t fresh-build .
  • Avoids using intermediate image cache layers

docs.title: Build Only Specific Targets (Multi-stage Builds)
docs.text:
Bash
docker build --target builder -t intermediate .

docs.title: Limit Image Size (Best Practices)
docs.text:
• Use minimal base images like alpine

  • Remove build dependencies after install

  • Use .dockerignore to reduce context

docs.title: View Build Output Verbosely
docs.text:
Bash
docker build --progress=plain .
  • Prints step-by-step output and environment details

docs.title: Shell Access During Build (Interactive Debugging)
docs.text:
Use temporary steps in Dockerfile:

Docker
RUN echo "Debug here" && sleep 3600

Then docker exec into the container in another shell (use another build process or dev container).

docs.title: Export and Analyze Image Layers
docs.text:
Bash
docker history myapp:latest
  • See size and command of each layer

docs.title: Inspect Intermediate Images
docs.text:
1. Enable buildkit:
Bash
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build .

  1. Add debug lines in Dockerfile with RUN sleep or RUN echo commands

  2. Use --target to stop at a specific build stage

docs.title: Use BuildKit Debugging Tools
docs.text:
Bash
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build --progress=plain .
  • Enables more detailed output and progress bars

docs.title: Lint and Validate Dockerfile
docs.text:
Use external tools like:

Bash
docker run --rm -i hadolint/hadolint < Dockerfile
  • Hadolint is a linter for Dockerfiles

docs.title: Use Temporary Containers to Test Commands
docs.text:
Bash
docker run -it --rm myimage /bin/sh

docs.title: Run Locally and Verify
docs.text:
Bash
docker run -it myapp:latest /bin/sh

docs.title: Automated Testing with docker-compose + CI/CD
docs.text:
• Define test services with health checks

  • Use docker-compose run or docker-compose exec to validate

docs.title: Save and Load Images
docs.text:
Bash
docker save myapp:latest -o myapp.tar
docker load -i myapp.tar

docs.title: Push to Registry
docs.text:
Bash
docker tag myapp:latest myregistry.com/myapp:latest
docker push myregistry.com/myapp:latest

docs.title: Docker Compose
docs.text:
## 📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-06-18 Updated Date: Wednesday 18th June 2025 10:54:39 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 📦 Docker Compose Examples and Use Cases
docs.text: Docker Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications. It allows you to configure services, networks, and volumes in a single docker-compose.yml file.

docs.title: Sample docker-compose.yml
docs.text:
YAML
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    image: nginx:latest
    ports:
      - "8080:80"

  app:
    build: ./app
    volumes:
      - ./app:/usr/src/app
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=development
    depends_on:
      - db

  db:
    image: postgres:13
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: pass
      POSTGRES_DB: mydb
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  pgdata:

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
Bash
docker-compose up         # Start all services
docker-compose down       # Stop and remove containers, networks, volumes
docker-compose build      # Build all services defined in the file
docker-compose ps         # List running services
docker-compose logs -f    # View logs

docs.title: 1. Development Environments
docs.text:
• Run backend, frontend, and database containers together.

  • Hot reload changes using volumes.

docs.title: 2. CI/CD Testing Pipelines
docs.text:
• Define disposable environments for integration tests.

  • Reproducible setups using same Compose config in test pipelines.

docs.title: 3. Service Dependencies
docs.text:
• Automatically manage dependencies (e.g., depends_on).

  • Use health checks to ensure readiness.

docs.title: 4. Local Replication of Production
docs.text: • Simulate production stack locally using the same Compose setup.

docs.title: 5. Database Migrations
docs.text: • Add migration containers with custom entrypoints to perform migrations at startup.

docs.title: 6. Multi-network Applications
docs.text: • Define multiple networks to isolate internal services from public-facing ones.

docs.title: Profiles (v3.9+)
docs.text:
YAML
services:
  debug:
    image: alpine
    command: sleep infinity
    profiles: ["debug"]
Bash
docker-compose --profile debug up

docs.title: Override Configuration
docs.text:
Bash
docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.override.yml up

docs.title: Resource Limits

services:

    deploy:

        limits:

docs.title: 🌐 Real-World Compose Template: Full Stack Example
docs.text:
YAML
version: '3.8'
services:
  frontend:
    build: ./frontend
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    depends_on:
      - backend

  backend:
    build: ./backend
    ports:
      - "5000:5000"
    environment:

    environment:

volumes:

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
## 📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-06-18 Updated Date: Tuesday 17th June 2025 21:52:05 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 🐳 Introduction to Docker
docs.text: Docker is an open-source platform designed to automate the deployment, scaling, and management of applications inside lightweight, portable containers. It enables developers and system administrators to package applications with all their dependencies and run them consistently across different environments.

docs.title: 🚀 Why Docker?
docs.text:
• Portability: Run the same container on any system that supports Docker (Linux, Windows, macOS).
  • Isolation: Containers encapsulate everything needed to run a service without affecting the host.
  • Efficiency: Shares the host OS kernel, making it faster and lighter than traditional virtual machines.
  • Version Control: Docker images can be versioned and stored in registries like Docker Hub or Harbor.
  • CI/CD Integration: Easily integrates with modern DevOps workflows.

docs.title: 🧩 Core Components of Docker
docs.text: Component Description Docker Engine The runtime that builds and runs containers. It includes the Docker daemon (dockerd), REST API, and CLI (docker). Images Immutable templates that contain the application and all dependencies. Created using Dockerfiles. Containers Running instances of images. Each container is isolated and stateless by default. Dockerfile Script used to build Docker images by defining steps, base images, and commands. Docker Compose Tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications using a docker-compose.yml file. Volumes Persistent storage mechanism used by containers to store data outside the container's filesystem. Networks Define communication between containers. Supports bridge, host, overlay, and custom networks. Docker Hub / Registry Repositories to store and share Docker images. Can be public (Docker Hub) or private (Harbor, GitLab, etc.).

docs.title: App Overview
docs.text:
## 📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-07-07 Updated Date: Monday 7th July 2025 19:13:07 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 🧱 App Overview: What We'll Build
docs.text:
A simple menu that lets the user choose an option using the arrow keys.

  • Press up/down to move the cursor.

  • Press enter to select.

docs.title: 🧭 Bubble Tea Architecture Overview
docs.text:
We'll walk through each piece:

Text Only
1. Model (holds state)
2. Msg (represents events)
3. Init() (optional setup, like timers or Cmds)
4. Update() (handles logic, changes model, returns Cmd)
5. View() (renders UI from model)
6. main() (entry point)

docs.title: 1️⃣ Model (📦 Application State)
docs.text:
This struct holds the current state of the app: choices, cursor, and selected value.

Go
type model struct {
    cursor   int      // current highlighted item
    choices  []string // menu options
    selected string   // user's final choice
}

docs.title: 📌 What is stored in state?
docs.text:
• The current cursor position (which menu item is selected).

  • The list of choices to display.

  • The selected item (after pressing enter).

docs.title: 2️⃣ Msg (📨 Messages or Events)
docs.text:
We don’t need to define custom messages here because keyboard events come as tea.KeyMsg (a built-in type from Bubble Tea).

You can think of this like:

  • "up" key → move cursor up

  • "down" key → move cursor down

  • "enter" key → select the option

Go
// No need for custom message yet; we use built-in tea.KeyMsg

docs.title: 3️⃣ Init() (🚀 App Initialization)
docs.text:
Runs once when the app starts. You can use it to return an initial Cmd (like a timer or async task). We don’t need that for now, so we return nil.

Go
func (m model) Init() tea.Cmd {
    return nil // no initial command needed
}

docs.title: 4️⃣ Update (🔁 Handles Events, Modifies State)
docs.text:
This is the brain of the application. It receives Msg (like a keypress) and updates the model.

Go
func (m model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    switch msg := msg.(type) {
    case tea.KeyMsg:
        switch msg.String() {
        case "up":
            if m.cursor > 0 {
                m.cursor--
            }

        case "down":
            if m.cursor < len(m.choices)-1 {
                m.cursor++
            }

        case "enter":
            m.selected = m.choices[m.cursor]
            return m, tea.Quit // select and quit app
        }
    }

    return m, nil
}

docs.title: ✅ What this does:
docs.text:
• Arrow keys change the cursor position.

  • Enter key saves the selected item and exits the app.

  • Returns updated model and (optionally) a command (Cmd).

docs.title: 5️⃣ View (🖼️ Renders UI from State)
docs.text:
This function draws the screen based on the current state.

Go
func (m model) View() string {
    s := "What do you want to do?\n\n"

    for i, choice := range m.choices {
        cursor := " " // default
        if m.cursor == i {
            cursor = ">" // highlight current item
        }

        s += fmt.Sprintf("%s %s\n", cursor, choice)
    }

    if m.selected != "" {
        s += fmt.Sprintf("\nYou selected: %s\n", m.selected)
    }

    return s
}

docs.title: 6️⃣ Main Function (🚪 Entry Point)
docs.text:
This puts everything together and runs the app.

Go
func main() {
    p := tea.NewProgram(model{
        choices: []string{"Create Bucket", "Delete Bucket", "List Buckets"},
    })

    if err := p.Start(); err != nil {
        fmt.Println("Error running program:", err)
        os.Exit(1)
    }
}

docs.title: ✅ Full Code in One File (for Copy-Paste)
🧠 Recap of Flow in Action
docs.text:
Text Only
1. App starts → Init() called → model initialized
2. User presses ↓ → KeyMsg sent → Update() runs
3. model.cursor updated → View() renders new screen
4. User presses ↵ Enter → Update() sets selection → View shows final message
5. tea.Quit stops the app

Would you like a version of this with Cmd for timers or background jobs, or would you prefer a quiz on how this architecture works?

docs.title: Architecture Flow
docs.text:
## 📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-07-07 Updated Date: Monday 7th July 2025 19:01:50 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 🧭 Big Picture: How Bubble Tea Works
docs.text: Bubble Tea uses a Model-Update-View (MUV) architecture — a variant of The Elm Architecture. This helps you write clean, reactive terminal apps.

docs.title: 🔁 The Event Loop in Bubble Tea
docs.text:
Text Only
User Input / Event
       ↓
      Msg
       ↓
  Update(model, msg)
       ↓
 New Model + Cmd
       ↓
   View(model)
       ↓
Render (Terminal Output)

Now let’s go through each of these in depth:

docs.title: 🔹 1. User Input / Events
docs.text:
❓ What is this? This includes:

  • Keyboard input (like pressing ↑ or ↓)

  • Mouse events

  • Timers (like a clock tick every second)

  • Network responses (if you're fetching data)

🎯 Goal: Detect that something happened.

🧠 You don’t process the input directly — instead, Bubble Tea transforms it into a Msg.

docs.title: 🔹 2. Msg (Message)
docs.text:
❓ What is this? It’s a type or struct that represents what happened.

You define a custom Msg type to represent user actions or system events.

✅ Example:

Go
type Msg string

const (
    MsgUp   Msg = "up"
    MsgDown Msg = "down"
)

Bubble Tea sends this Msg into the next stage.

docs.title: 🔹 3. Update(model, msg)
docs.text:
❓ What is this? The Update function handles logic.

You write logic inside Update to:

  • Change the state (i.e., update the model)

  • Optionally trigger side effects like timers or network calls (via Cmd)

✅ Example:

Go
func (m model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (model, tea.Cmd) {
    switch msg := msg.(type) {
    case tea.KeyMsg:
        switch msg.String() {
        case "up":
            m.cursor--
        case "down":
            m.cursor++
        }
    }
    return m, nil
}

🎯 Goal: Return a new model (updated state) and optionally a command (Cmd).

docs.title: 🔹 4. New Model + Cmd
docs.text:
✅ Model: The new application state after handling the message.

✅ Cmd (Command): Tells Bubble Tea to run something asynchronously and return a new Msg later.

Common use cases for Cmd:

  • Wait for a timer and send a tick

  • Make a network request

  • Read a file

  • Animate something

✅ Example:

Go
return m, tea.Tick(time.Second, func(t time.Time) tea.Msg {
    return tickMsg(t)
})

docs.title: 🔹 5. View(model)
docs.text:
❓ What is this? It’s a function that takes your model (state) and returns a string to render in the terminal.

🎯 Goal: Turn state into user interface.

✅ Example:

Go
func (m model) View() string {
    s := "Choose an option:\n\n"
    for i, choice := range m.choices {
        cursor := " " // no cursor
        if m.cursor == i {
            cursor = ">" // highlight
        }
        s += fmt.Sprintf("%s %s\n", cursor, choice)
    }
    return s
}

docs.title: 🔄 Loop Back
docs.text: After rendering, Bubble Tea waits for the next event or message, and the cycle repeats.

docs.title: 🎨 Visual Flow Summary
docs.text:
Text Only
1. User presses key          ⌨️
2. Bubble Tea converts to →  Msg
3. Msg sent to →             Update()
4. Update changes →          Model + optional Cmd
5. View() renders →          New UI based on state
6. Back to waiting for event...

docs.title: 🔍 Real Example Flow
docs.text: Let’s say the user presses the down arrow key.

docs.title: Step-by-step:
docs.text:
1. Key press: "down"

  2. Converted to: tea.KeyMsg{Type: KeyDown}

  3. Update() sees the "down" key and increments m.cursor

  4. New model with updated cursor

  5. View() highlights the next item in the list

  6. Terminal redraws the new screen

docs.title: 🧠 Why This Architecture is Powerful:
docs.text:
• Separation of Concerns: View is only for display. Update is only for logic.

  • Testable: You can test each function easily.

  • Async Friendly: Cmd makes async logic simple without goroutines everywhere.

  • Declarative UI: You describe what the UI should look like based on state.

docs.title: TUI Development Guide
docs.text:
## 📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-07-07 Updated Date: Monday 7th July 2025 12:05:28 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: Phase 1: Understanding Bubbletea & Lipgloss Architecture (Today)
docs.text: We'll begin with the fundamentals of how Bubbletea and Lipgloss work under the hood:

docs.title: 📚 BUBBLETEA — Architecture Overview
docs.text: Bubbletea is based on The Elm Architecture, which is a pattern for managing state and effects in a functional, reactive way.

docs.title: 🔁 Core Concepts
docs.text: Concept Description Model Holds the state of the application. Msg Represents events (user input, timers, commands). Init() Initializes the app and can return initial commands. Update() Handles Msgs, updates the Model, optionally returns a Cmd. View() Renders the UI as a string based on the current Model. Cmd Asynchronous tasks like HTTP requests, time.Sleep, etc.

docs.title: 📘 Minimal Diagram:
docs.text:
Text Only
User Input / Events
        ↓
     Msg (message)
        ↓
    Update(model, msg)
        ↓
   New Model + Cmd (optional async)
        ↓
     View(model) → string → Rendered to Terminal

You write:

  • Model: structure of your app’s state

  • Update: how the state changes when events come in

  • View: what the user sees based on the current state

docs.title: 🎨 LIPGLOSS — Styling Your Terminal UI
docs.text: Lipgloss is like CSS for your terminal UI. It styles the string output (from View() in Bubbletea).

docs.title: 🎨 Key Concepts:
docs.text:
Concept Purpose lipgloss.NewStyle() Creates a new style chain. .Foreground(), .Background() Set text and background colors. .Bold(), .Italic(), .Underline() Text formatting. .Margin(), .Padding(), .Width() Layout helpers. .Render("text") Applies styles to text.

You use Lipgloss inside the View() function in Bubbletea.

docs.title: 🔧 Putting it Together: Mini Skeleton
🔜 Your Immediate Next Steps:
docs.text:
1. Learn by Doing: I’ll guide you in writing a very basic but visually styled TUI app (counter with up/down buttons and Lipgloss styling).

  2. Break Down Core Interfaces:

    • How Model, Msg, Cmd, tea.Program work in Go.

  3. Component-based TUI building: Like “pages” in a TUI or reusable widgets.

docs.title: Tea.Cmd
docs.text:
## 📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-07-07 Updated Date: Monday 7th July 2025 19:18:58 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 📦 What is tea.Cmd?
docs.text:
In Bubble Tea:

Go
type Cmd func() Msg

That means a Cmd is just a function that returns a Msg.

So, when you return a tea.Cmd from either:

  • the Init() function, or

  • the Update() function,

you're telling Bubble Tea:

“Please run this code asynchronously and send its result back into my program as a Msg.”

It's Bubble Tea’s way of handling side effects, like:

  • Fetching data from a file or the network

  • Waiting for a timer

  • Running a background task

  • Listening for input

  • Any asynchronous work that will eventually send data back to your program

docs.title: 🧠 Why is Init() defined like this?
✅ Example with tea.Cmd in Init()
docs.text:
Let's say you want to wait 2 seconds and then show a message.

Go
func (m model) Init() tea.Cmd {
    return tea.Tick(2*time.Second, func(t time.Time) tea.Msg {
        return msgTimePassed{}
    })
}

Here:

  • tea.Tick waits for 2 seconds,

  • then sends msgTimePassed{} to the Update() function,

  • which can then update your model and re-render the screen.

docs.title: 📝 Summary
docs.text:
Function Purpose Return tea.Cmd to... Init() Called once at startup Start async/background work Update() Called on every Msg Trigger new async work View() Returns a string Never returns Cmd

So tea.Cmd is how Bubble Tea does side-effects in a clean, functional way.

docs.title: 📂 Project Setup Steps
docs.text: Step Command Description 1️⃣ echo "# Automation" >> README.md Creates a README.md file with the title "Automation". 2️⃣ git init Initializes a new Git repository in the current directory. 3️⃣ git add README.md Stages the README.md file for the first commit. 4️⃣ git commit -m "first commit" Commits the staged file with a message. 5️⃣ git branch -M master Renames the default branch to master. 6️⃣ git remote add origin git@github.com:BiratAryal/Automation.git Sets the remote repository URL. 7️⃣ git push -u origin master Pushes the code to GitHub and sets upstream tracking.

docs.title: Merge Strategy
docs.text:
## 📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-06-18 Updated Date: Wednesday 18th June 2025 10:52:13 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 🧠 Git Merging Strategies - Detailed Documentation
docs.text: Comprehensive guide to Git merge strategies, including definitions, command examples, editor behavior, commit history implications, and practical workflows.

docs.title: 🧭 Introduction
docs.text: Git merge strategies determine how changes from one branch are integrated into another. The right strategy depends on whether you want to retain detailed history, squash it, or ignore it entirely.

docs.title: ❓ Why Merging Strategies Matter
docs.text:
• 🔍 Traceable project history

  • 🧘‍♀️ Clean, readable logs

  • 🛠️ Efficient conflict resolution

  • 🤝 Better team collaboration

docs.title: 1. Recursive (Default)
docs.text:
Usage: Most common; works with two branches that have diverged.

Bash
git merge feature/login

docs.title: 2. Resolve
docs.text:
Usage: Simple, two-parent merge; mostly deprecated in favor of recursive.

Bash
git merge -s resolve feature/login

docs.title: 3. Octopus
docs.text:
Usage: Merge multiple branches (no conflicts allowed).

Bash
git merge -s octopus feature1 feature2 feature3

docs.title: 4. Ours
docs.text:
Usage: Use current branch's changes and discard the other.

Bash
git merge -s ours experiment-branch

docs.title: 5. Subtree
docs.text:
Usage: Merging unrelated projects while keeping their directory structures.

Bash
git merge -s subtree project-b

docs.title: 6. Squash ✅
docs.text:
Usage: Flatten all commits from a feature branch into one commit. Great for clean history and PR-based workflows.

Bash
git merge --squash feature/checkout-page

🛠 What happens:

  • Git collects all commits from the feature branch.

  • The code from all those commits is merged into your working directory.

  • A single commit is created (but not yet committed).

You’ll see a text editor (usually Vim or VSCode) open with something like:

Text Only
# This is a combination of 5 commits.
# The first commit's message is:
Add checkout page UI

# The 2nd commit message is:
Implement discount logic

# The 3rd commit message is:
Refactor checkout controller

# Please enter the commit message for your changes.

📝 You can edit this message, summarizing all changes in a single commit:

Text Only
Add complete checkout functionality

- Added UI for checkout
- Implemented coupon/discount logic
- Refactored controller logic

Then commit it manually:

Bash
git commit

🔁 Final result: One new commit is added to the main branch — a flattened, clean version of the entire feature.

🧩 Pro Tip:

  • Combine with git rebase -i to refine history before squashing.

  • Use in PR-based workflows for atomic merges.

docs.title: Fast-forward (--ff)
docs.text:
• Pointer just moves forward (no merge commit).
Strategy Description --ff Moves the branch pointer ahead—no merge commit --no-ff Forces a merge commit—keeps merge info Bash
git merge --ff feature

docs.title: No Fast-forward (--no-ff)
docs.text:
• Always creates a merge commit.
Bash
git merge --no-ff feature

docs.title: 🌍 Real-world Use Cases
docs.text: Scenario Strategy Why Feature branch merge --no-ff Keeps history of the feature Hotfix multiple branches octopus Merge all hotfixes at once Discard experimental code ours Keep current state, mark merge complete Vendor code or repo fusion subtree Preserve separate history in a sub-dir Simplify history for PR --squash Single meaningful commit on main branch Rewriting messy local commits rebase -i Makes feature history clean

docs.title: 📊 Merge vs Squash vs Rebase – Comparison
docs.text: Feature / Goal merge --squash rebase octopus ours Preserve commit history ✅ Yes ❌ No (one commit) ✅ Yes (rewritten) ✅ Yes ❌ No Creates merge commit ✅ Yes ❌ No ❌ No ✅ Yes ✅ Yes (but fake) Used in PR workflows ✅ Common ✅ Very common ⚠️ Sometimes ⚠️ Rarely ❌ No Conflict resolution ✅ Supported ✅ At squash time ✅ During rebase ❌ Not supported ❌ Discards changes Interactive history cleanup ❌ No ⚠️ Limited ✅ Yes (with -i) ❌ No ❌ No Automated multi-branch ❌ No ❌ No ❌ No ✅ Yes ❌ No Final commit behavior Merge + all commits One single commit Rewritten commits Merge of many “Keep ours” only Best for Team collaboration Clean history Linear development Release branches Ignoring other branch

docs.title: GitHub / GitLab Merge Requests (PR Model)
docs.text:
Bash
# Checkout main
git checkout main

# Merge feature cleanly
git merge --squash feature/login
git commit -m "Add user login functionality"

Use --squash in conjunction with CI pipelines and PR reviews to reduce commit noise and keep history readable.

docs.title: 🚧 Interactive Rebase
docs.text:
Bash
git rebase -i HEAD~5
# Reorder, squash, or fixup commits before merging

docs.title: ✅ Best Practices
docs.text:
• 📌 Use --no-ff when you want to preserve merge context.

  • 🧹 Use --squash to clean messy commits before merging to main.

  • 🔀 Avoid octopus if there's any chance of a conflict.

  • 🚫 Use ours sparingly—you're discarding someone’s work.

  • 🧰 Document your team strategy in CONTRIBUTING.md.

docs.title: ☸️ Kubernetes Introduction
docs.text: Kubernetes (often abbreviated as K8s) is an open-source container orchestration platform designed to automate the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. Originally developed by Google and now maintained by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), Kubernetes has become the industry standard for managing containerized workloads.

docs.title: 🧩 Why Kubernetes?
docs.text:
Kubernetes solves many common problems in modern cloud-native deployments:

  • 🔁 Automated Rollouts & Rollbacks: Deploy changes gradually and automatically revert if issues are detected.

  • 📦 Service Discovery & Load Balancing: Distribute traffic evenly across healthy pods.

  • ⚖️ Horizontal Scaling: Automatically scale applications based on CPU/memory or custom metrics.

  • ♻️ Self-healing: Replace failed containers, reschedule pods on healthy nodes.

  • 🗂️ Config & Secrets Management: Decouple environment-specific configuration from code.

  • 🔒 RBAC & Network Policies: Secure workloads through fine-grained access control and isolation.

docs.title: 📁 Core Concepts
docs.text:
• Pod: The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, which can contain one or more containers.

  • Service: An abstraction that defines a logical set of pods and a policy to access them.

  • Deployment: Manages replica sets to maintain a desired number of pod replicas.

  • ReplicaSet: Ensures a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time.

  • ConfigMap: Stores non-sensitive configuration data.

  • Secret: Stores sensitive information like passwords, tokens, or SSH keys.

  • Namespace: Provides logical isolation of resources.

  • Volume: Persistent storage abstraction used by pods.

docs.title: 🌎 Kubernetes Use Cases
docs.text:
• Microservice architectures

  • Continuous deployment (CI/CD)

  • Multi-cloud and hybrid deployments

  • Serverless computing (via KNative, for example)

  • Infrastructure abstraction and resource optimization

docs.title: 🐚 Bash Scripting for Beginners
docs.text: This guide is a starting point for learning Bash scripting on Linux. It covers basic syntax, variables, conditionals, loops, and useful examples to get you going.

docs.title: 📝 What is Bash?
docs.text: Bash (Bourne Again SHell) is a Unix shell and command language. Bash scripts allow you to automate tasks, chain commands, and create custom tools.

docs.title: 🔹 Create a Bash Script
docs.text:
1. Open a terminal.

  2. Create a file:

Bash
touch hello.sh
  1. Make it executable:
Bash
chmod +x hello.sh
  1. Add a shebang at the top of the file:
Bash
#!/bin/bash

/bin/bash denotes the shell which would be used.

docs.title: 🔤 Basic Script Example
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "Hello, World!"

Run it:

Bash
./hello.sh

docs.title: 🔧 Variables
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
name="Birat"
echo "Hello, $name!"

docs.title: 📥 Read User Input
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "What's your name? "
read name
echo "Welcome, $name!"

Tip

You could use on single line to get input from user like this:

Bash
#!/bin/bash
read -p "What's your name? " name
echo "Welcome, $name!"

docs.title: 🔄 Conditionals
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "Enter a number:"
read num

if [ "$num" -gt 10 ]; then
    echo "Number is greater than 10"
else
    echo "Number is 10 or less"
fi

Syntax

Bash
if [ condition ]; then
    # if-block
else
    # else-block
fi

docs.title: For Loop
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
    echo "Number: $i"
done

Syntax

Bash
for variable in list
do
    commands
done

docs.title: While Loop
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
count=1
while [ $count -le 5 ]; do
    echo "Count is $count"
    count=$((count + 1))
done

Syntax

Bash
while [ condition ]
do
    commands
done

docs.title: 📂 File Check Example
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
file="/etc/passwd"

if [ -f "$file" ]; then
    echo "$file exists."
else
    echo "$file does not exist."
fi

docs.title: 📂 Directory Listing with Loop
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for file in *.txt; do
    echo "Found text file: $file"
done

docs.title: 🔧 Functions
📜 Command Line Arguments
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "Script name: $0"
echo "First argument: $1"
echo "Second argument: $2"

Run with:

Bash
./script.sh one two

docs.title: 🚦 Exit Status
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
ls /etc/passwd
echo "Exit status: $?"

docs.title: 🔁 Sample Real Script: Backup Files
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
src_dir="/home/user/docs"
backup_dir="/home/user/backup"

mkdir -p "$backup_dir"
cp -r "$src_dir"/* "$backup_dir"

echo "Backup complete!"

Tips

  • Use chmod +x script.sh to make your script executable.

  • Use set -x to debug your script.

  • Always double quotes variables: "$var"

  • Single quotes does not return the value defined for variables: '$var'

  • Test with bash -n script.sh for syntax checking.

docs.title: 🔁 Bash for Loops
docs.text: The for loop in Bash is used to iterate over a list of items, numeric ranges, or file patterns.

docs.title: 📐 Basic Syntax
docs.text:
Bash
for variable in list
do
    # commands
done

docs.title: 1. Loop Over a List of Words
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for name in Alice Bob Charlie
do
    echo "Hello, $name!"
done

docs.title: 2. Loop Over a Range of Numbers (Brace Expansion)
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for i in {1..5}
do
    echo "Number: $i"
done

docs.title: 3. Loop Over Files
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for file in *.txt
do
    echo "Found file: $file"
done

docs.title: 4. Loop Over Command Output
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for user in $(cut -d: -f1 /etc/passwd)
do
    echo "User: $user"
done

docs.title: 🧱 Nested for Loops
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for i in 1 2 3
do
    for j in a b
    do
        echo "i=$i, j=$j"
    done
done

docs.title: Example: Skip an Item with continue
docs.text:
Bash
for i in {1..5}
do
    if [ $i -eq 3 ]; then
        continue
    fi
    echo "i=$i"
done

docs.title: Example: Exit Early with break
docs.text:
Bash
for i in {1..5}
do
    if [ $i -eq 3 ]; then
        break
    fi
    echo "i=$i"
done

docs.title: 🔄 Combining with Conditionals
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for i in {1..10}
do
    if (( i % 2 == 0 )); then
        echo "$i is even"
    else
        echo "$i is odd"
    fi
done

docs.title: 📦 Loop Over Arguments ($@)
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for arg in "$@"
do
    echo "Argument: $arg"
done

Run with:

Bash
./script.sh apple banana cherry

🧠 Tips

  • Use quotes ("$var") to handle spaces

  • Use [[ ... ]] for safer condition checks

  • Use set -x to debug loop behavior

docs.title: 🧠 Bash if Conditionals
docs.text: The if statement in Bash is used to perform conditional execution of commands. It forms the foundation for decision-making in scripts.

docs.title: 📐 Basic Syntax
docs.text:
Bash
if [ condition ]; then
    # commands
fi

docs.title: With else
docs.text:
Bash
if [ condition ]; then
    # if-block
else
    # else-block
fi

docs.title: With elif (else-if)
docs.text:
Bash
if [ condition1 ]; then
    # commands
elif [ condition2 ]; then
    # other commands
else
    # fallback
fi

docs.title: Numeric
docs.text: Operator Meaning -eq equal -ne not equal -lt less than -le less than or equal -gt greater than -ge greater than or equal

docs.title: String
docs.text: Operator Meaning = or == equal != not equal -z is empty -n is not empty

docs.title: File
docs.text: Operator Meaning -f file file exists and is a regular file -d dir directory exists -e path file or directory exists -r readable -w writable -x executable

docs.title: 1. Numeric Comparison
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
num=5
if [ $num -gt 3 ]; then
    echo "$num is greater than 3"
fi

docs.title: 2. String Comparison
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
name="Birat"
if [ "$name" = "Birat" ]; then
    echo "Welcome, $name!"
else
    echo "Access denied"
fi

docs.title: 3. Check if File Exists
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
if [ -f "/etc/passwd" ]; then
    echo "File exists"
else
    echo "File not found"
fi

docs.title: 4. Nested if Statement
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
num=10
if [ $num -gt 0 ]; then
    if [ $num -lt 100 ]; then
        echo "$num is between 1 and 99"
    fi
fi

docs.title: 5. if with Logical Operators
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
age=25
if [ $age -gt 18 ] && [ $age -lt 30 ]; then
    echo "Eligible"
fi

Other logic options:

  • && (AND)

  • || (OR)

  • ! (NOT)

docs.title: 🔁 if in Loops (Example)
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
for num in 1 2 3 4 5
do
    if [ $num -eq 3 ]; then
        echo "Three found!"
    else
        echo "$num"
    fi
done

Tips

Use [[ ... ]] for safer conditions

Bash
if [[ $string == "value" ]]; then
    echo "Match"
fi

docs.text:
📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-06-23 Updated Date: Monday 23rd June 2025 07:36:09 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: Log Rotation Configuration for Servicename
docs.text:
This file defines the log rotation policy for the Servicename service. The configuration is located at:

Text Only
/etc/logrotate.d/Servicename

docs.title: 📄 Configuration Overview
docs.text:
Bash
<location of log>/*.log {
    daily
    create <Permission with umask> <username> <group>
    dateext
    compress
    ifempty
    missingok
    copytruncate
    rotate 7
    postrotate
        # Remove rotated files older than 7 days
        find <location of logs> -type f -mtime +7 -name '*.gz' -delete
    endscript
}

docs.title: 🔧 Explanation of Options
docs.text: Directive Description <location of log>/*.log Path to log files that will be rotated (e.g., /var/log/servicename/*.log) daily Rotate logs on a daily basis create Create a new empty log file after rotation with specific permissions and ownership. Replace <Permission with umask>, <username>, and <group> accordingly (e.g., 0640 servicename adm) dateext Adds the current date to the rotated log filenames instead of a numerical suffix compress Compresses the old log files using gzip ifempty Rotate log files even if they are empty missingok Don't show an error if the log file is missing copytruncate Truncates the original log file after copying it, used for active logs written by running processes rotate 7 Keep the last 7 rotated log files before deletion postrotate ... endscript Executes a script after rotation; here it deletes compressed log files (.gz) older than 7 days

docs.title: 🧹 Log Cleanup
docs.text:
After each rotation, this command will delete compressed (*.gz) log files older than 7 days:

Bash
find <location of logs> -type f -mtime +7 -name '*.gz' -delete

Replace <location of logs> with the correct log directory path (e.g., /var/log/servicename).

docs.title: ✅ Example
docs.text:
If your logs are located at /var/log/servicename/ and owned by user servicename in group adm, an example config might look like:

Bash
/var/log/servicename/*.log {
    daily
    create 0640 servicename adm
    dateext
    compress
    ifempty
    missingok
    copytruncate
    rotate 7
    postrotate
        find /var/log/servicename -type f -mtime +7 -name '*.gz' -delete
    endscript
}

docs.title: 🛠️ Apply and Test
docs.text:
To test this configuration manually:

Bash
logrotate -d /etc/logrotate.d/Servicename   # Dry run
logrotate -f /etc/logrotate.d/Servicename   # Force rotation

docs.title: While Condition
docs.text:
📝 Author

Birat Aryal — birataryal.github.io Created Date: 2025-06-23 Updated Date: Monday 23rd June 2025 08:16:15 Website - birataryal.com.np Repository - Birat Aryal LinkedIn - Birat Aryal DevSecOps Engineer | System Engineer | Cyber Security Analyst | Network Engineer

docs.title: 🔁 Bash while Loops
docs.text: The while loop in Bash repeatedly executes a block of commands as long as the condition is true.

docs.title: 📐 Basic Syntax
docs.text:
Bash
while [ condition ]
do
    # commands
done

The condition is checked before each iteration.

docs.title: 1. Basic Counter Example
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
count=1
while [ $count -le 5 ]
do
    echo "Count: $count"
    ((count++))  # increment
done

docs.title: 2. Countdown Timer
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
n=5
while [ $n -gt 0 ]
do
    echo "$n..."
    sleep 1
    ((n--))
done
echo "Go!"

docs.title: 3. User Input Until Match
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
input=""
while [ "$input" != "yes" ]
do
    echo "Type 'yes' to continue:"
    read input
done
echo "Thanks!"

docs.title: 4. Read a File Line by Line
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
file="/etc/passwd"

while IFS= read -r line
do
    echo "$line"
done < "$file"

docs.title: 5. Infinite Loop
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
while true
do
    echo "Running..."
    sleep 2
done

Stop with Ctrl + C.

docs.title: 🧱 Nested while Loops
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
i=1
while [ $i -le 3 ]
do
    j=1
    while [ $j -le 2 ]
    do
        echo "i=$i, j=$j"
        ((j++))
    done
    ((i++))
done

docs.title: ⛔ Exit Conditions
docs.text: Use break to exit early and continue to skip an iteration.

docs.title: Example: break
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
i=1
while [ $i -le 5 ]
do
    if [ $i -eq 3 ]; then
        break
    fi
    echo "i=$i"
    ((i++))
done

docs.title: Example: continue
docs.text:
Bash
#!/bin/bash
i=0
while [ $i -lt 5 ]
do
    ((i++))
    if [ $i -eq 3 ]; then
        continue
    fi
    echo "$i"
done

🔐 Safe Practices

  • Quote variables: "$var"

  • Use [[ ... ]] for complex logic

  • Test loops with set -x for debugging

docs.title: 📘 MinIO Management Guide Using mc (MinIO Client)
docs.text: This document provides a comprehensive reference for managing a MinIO server using the mc client tool. It assumes that: - MinIO server is running and accessible. - An alias localmino is configured for the MinIO instance.

docs.title: 1. Install mc (MinIO Client)
docs.text:
Download from: https://min.io/download#/mc

Then install:

Bash
chmod +x mc
sudo mv mc /usr/local/bin/

docs.title: 2. Add MinIO Server as Alias
docs.text:
If not already added:

Bash
mc alias set localmino http://<minio-server-ip>:9000 <ACCESS_KEY> <SECRET_KEY>

✅ Example:

Bash
mc alias set localmino http://192.168.150.14:9000 minioadmin minioadmin

docs.title: 🔹 List All Buckets
docs.text:
Bash
mc ls localmino

docs.title: 🔹 Create a New Bucket
🔹 Remove a Bucket
docs.text:
Bash
mc rb localmino/<bucket-name>

Bucket must be empty to be deleted.

docs.title: 🔺 Upload Files to a Bucket
docs.text:
Bash
mc cp /path/to/file localmino/<bucket-name>/

✅ Example:

Bash
mc cp mydata.zip localmino/user-backups/

docs.title: 🔻 Download Files from a Bucket
docs.text:
Bash
mc cp localmino/<bucket-name>/<file> /destination/path/

✅ Example:

Bash
mc cp localmino/user-backups/mydata.zip .

docs.title: Synchronize Local Directory to Bucket
docs.text:
Bash
mc mirror /local/dir/ localmino/<bucket-name>/

✅ Example:

Bash
mc mirror /data/uploads localmino/user-backups/

docs.title: Synchronize Bucket to Local
docs.text:
Bash
mc mirror localmino/<bucket-name>/ /local/dir/

docs.title: 👤 User and Policy Management
docs.text: For IAM-compatible setups (MinIO in server mode with identity support).

docs.title: 🔹 Add a New User
🔹 Enable/Disable User
🔹 Remove a User

Bash
mc admin user remove localmino <username>

docs.title: 🔹 Create a Custom Policy (From JSON)
docs.text:
Bash
mc admin policy add localmino <policy-name> /path/to/policy.json

✅ Example:

Bash
mc admin policy add localmino readonly /home/admin/readonly.json

docs.title: 🔹 Assign Policy to a User
🔹 List All Policies
docs.text:
Bash
mc admin policy list localmino

docs.title: 🔸 View Server Info
docs.text:
Bash
mc admin info localmino

docs.title: 🔸 Check Disk Usage
docs.text:
Bash
mc du localmino/<bucket-name>

docs.title: 🔁 Watch for Changes in Bucket
docs.text:
Bash
mc watch localmino/<bucket-name>

docs.title: 📋 List Files Recursively
docs.text:
Bash
mc find localmino/<bucket-name> --name "*"

docs.title: 🧰 Example: Full Setup for New User
docs.text:
Bash
# 1. Create a bucket
mc mb localmino/projects

# 2. Add a user
mc admin user add localmino developer1 Dev123Secure!

# 3. Create a readwrite policy (assuming JSON exists)
mc admin policy add localmino rw-policy ./readwrite-policy.json

# 4. Assign policy to user
mc admin policy set localmino rw-policy user=developer1

docs.title: 📎 Sample Policy: Read-Only Access
docs.text:
Save as readonly.json:

JSON
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:ListBucket"],
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Resource": ["arn:aws:s3:::user-backups/*", "arn:aws:s3:::user-backups"]
    }
  ]
